name: HMS Unbending (P 37)
events: The history of HMS P 37 / HMS Unbending as compiled on this page is extracted from the patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine and was created in October 2014. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in May 2026.
4 Nov 1941 Around 1215 A/4, P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). (1)

5 Nov 1941 At 1550 A/5, P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Barrow. En-route various trials were carried out including acceptance trials. At Holy Loch the submarine was officially taken over from the shipyard and placed in full commission.
HMS P 37 now commenced a work-up period. (1)
7 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted exercises in Loch Long. (1)
8 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted exercises in Loch Long. (1)
9 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
10 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
12 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Arrochar where torpedo firing trials were to be carried out on the torpedo range. (1)
13 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
14 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
15 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
16 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
17 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
18 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) proceeded from Arrochar to Holy Loch. (1)
19 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted gunnery and W/T calibration trials in the Clyde area. (1)
20 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Long. (1)
22 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN) acted as target. (1)
23 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN) acted as target. (1)
24 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) is docked in AFD 7 at Rothesay. (1)
25 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) is undocked at Rothesay. She then conducted gunnery exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
26 Nov 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Holy Loch. (1)
27 Nov 1941 Around 1215 A/27, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Holy Loch for Scapa Flow. She was escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). (1)
29 Nov 1941 Around 0930 A/29, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow to participate in A/S exercises.
It appears that HMS P 37 conducted (A/S) exercises at / off Scapa Flow on 1, 3 and 4 December.
[No further details available on these exercises as no logs are available of HMS P 37 for the period December 1941 - June 1942.] (2)
5 Dec 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Scapa Flow for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off central Norway.
She was escorted out to the east of the Shetland Islands by HMS Stella Pegasi (T/Lt. F.S. Howell, RNR).
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed. Also no patrol report is available so details for this patrol will be missing.] (3)

6 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2152 A/6, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); The Swedish merchant vessels Glimmaren (Swedish, 3173 GRT, built 1939) and Bardaland (Swedish, 2956 GRT, built 1936) have sailed from Goteborg routed through position 62°00'N, 03°00'E due Faeroes A.M./8. If encountered on this route these ships should not be molested. (4)

12 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1305 A/12, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); Leave patrol after dark today through the following positions [not included in the signal] diving by day to make rendezvous in position 60°25'N, 00°30'W at 1000 A/14 for onward escort to Scapa. (4)
14 Dec 1941 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Lerwick. (5)
15 Dec 1941 Around 1700 A/15, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Lerwick for Scapa Flow. She was escorted by HMS Wheatland (Lt. R.deL. Brooke, RN).
HMS Wheatland had departed Scapa Flow for this escort duty at 0840 B/15. (6)
16 Dec 1941 Around 1400 A/16, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) and her escort HMS Wheatland (Lt. R.deL. Brooke, RN), arrived at Scapa Flow from Lerwick.
At Scapa Flow HMS P 37 was again to participate in (A/S) exercises. [No further details available.] (6)

2 Jan 1942 HMS Nigeria (Capt. J.G.L. Dundas, CBE, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral H.M. Burrough, CB, RN) conducted A/S exercises at Scapa Flow during which HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) served as target. (7)
13 Jan 1942 Around 1500 A/13, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Scapa Flow for Dundee. She was escorted by HMS Scalby Wyke (Skr. C.A. Grimmer, RNR). (8)
14 Jan 1942 In the morning, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived at Dundee from Scapa Flow. (8)
28 Jan 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Dundee for Lerwick. She was escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (8)
29 Jan 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived at Lerwick from Dundee. (8)
10 Feb 1942 At 1700 A/10, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Lerwick for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off Norway, Kristiansund area.

She was to proceed on the surface, unescorted, through positions 'A' ( 90° - Muckle Flugga - 10 miles ), 'B' (61°30'N, 00°30'W), 'C' (65°00'N, 01°00'E) thence submerged by day through 'D' (65°00'N, 08°20'E) to patrol in zone 'K 7' area 'W'.

During this patrol, the Channel Dash occurred and the submarine was ordered not to attack anything less than cruiser and to watch the approaches of Trondheim with HMS Trident, HMS Tuna and the Free French FFS Minerve. On 23 February, HMS Trident torpedoed the German heavy cruiser Prinz Eugen but P 37 did not observe anything.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (9)

12 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1517 A/12, the Flag Officer Submarines sent the following orders to HMS Tigris (Lt. L.W. Napier, RN) and HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); Proceed forthwith to patrol in vicinity of positions 63°22'N, 07°30'E and 63°14'N, 07°07'E respectively. (10)
13 Feb 1942 At 2000 A/13, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived in the ordered patrol area near position 63°14'N, 07°07'E (between Bud and Kristansund).
At 2100 A/13, the working lights of fishing trawlers were sighted which remained in the area all night. P 37 kept clear as necessary. (11)
14 Feb 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) sighted only some small fishing drifters in her assigned patrol area on this day. (11)

15 Feb 1942 At 0935 A/15, to the east of the Grip lighthouse to the north of Kristiansund, Norway, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) sighted a convoy of three merchant vessels escorted by three trawlers bearing 230° at a range of 10000 yards proceeding on course 040° at 9 knots.
A dummy attack, in which the range was not closed to less then 3000 yards, was subsequently carried out as P 37 had orders not to attack anything less than a cruiser.

[We have been unable to positively identify this convoy. A northbound convoy made up of the transports Hans Bornhofen (German, 2130 GRT, built 1905), Tagila (German, 2621 GRT, built 1906) and Motta (Finnish, 1173 GRT, built 1903) escorted by the auxiliary patrol vessels Eupen and NM 24 had departed Alesund around 0800 B/15. We doubt if these could be this far north in 2.5 hours (0935 A/15 was the first sighting). It is however possible that an error in time has been made somewhere.]

At 1002 A/15, an explosion was heard fairly close. No further unfriendly activity was noticed though but a large trawler was seen at a range of about 4000 yards, close to the Grip lighthouse steering for Grip Holm.

The ships of the convoy had been estimated to be a 4000-5000 ton cargo ship, a ship of about 2000 ton which looked a bit like a cross-channel steamer and another vessel of about 2000 ton, thought to be of the tramp type but this vessel was only seen indistinctly. The trawlers of the escort were small and looked more like ocean-going tugs. The trawler seen later looked like the British Lord Lloyd-class.
At 1900 A/15, HMS P 37 surfaced to charge six miles to the north of the Grip lighthouse so as to avoid the fishing vessels to the south.
At 1930 A/15, lights were seen thought to be fishing vessels.
At 1950 A/15, one of the fishing vessels started flashing. P 37 dived and withdrew further to the north.
At 2100 A/15, P 37 surfaced. Nothing was in sight.

At 2105 A/15, a searchlight was seen on bearing 220°. It was sweeping at intervals. P 37 was out of range of this searchlight but to be sure withdrew further to the north as a precaution. It was considered this searchlight was near Kristiansund or possibly on Grip Island. (12)

16 Feb 1942 At 0740 A/16, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN), which was proceeding dived towards the Grip lighthouse, struck the bottom at 60 feet. Speed was 2 knots at the time. P 37 then was brought up to periscope depth and subsequently taken into deeper water.
She then patrolled in the vicinity of 270° - Grip lighthouse - 4 miles. Only some fishing vessels were seen throughout the day until ...

1720 A/16, when a convoy was sighted entering Kristiansund in the fading light. The convoy came from the south, in approximately the same position as the convoy seen the previous day. Range on sighting was 7000 yards. An escort of two trawlers, resembling ocean-going tugs, was present. The convoy was made up of two merchant vessels and possibly a third. Lt. Winter did not close as no attacks were allowed on targets smaller then a cruiser.
[We have been unable to positively identify this convoy.]

At 1900 A/16, HMS P 37 withdrew to the north to comply with the Flag Officers Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 1224 A/16 which contained the following orders; Patrol about 18 miles off the coast between Griptarene and Ulla 62°40'N. Your object is to attack and then report cruiser and above. It is of utmost importance that your presence remain undisclosed. (9)

18 Feb 1942 At 0230 A/18, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) proceeded in accordance with the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 0026 A/18 which contained the following orders; Proceed forthwith diving by day to patrol within five miles of position 63°40'N, 08°00'E.
At 0620 A/18, HMS P 37 dived in position 63°40'N, 07°45'E.
At 1400 A/18, HMS P 37 sighted land but due to the poor visibility had difficulties fixing her exact position but it was considered that she was in the correct position though.
At 1600 A/18, a trawler was sighted at a range of 4000 yards. The trawler was seen to be fishing.
At 1700 A/18, A/S impulses was heard as was HE. This HE had been heard for some time and it was considered that another submarine was in the area.
At 1702 A/18, HMS P 37 was challenged on S.S./T. by HMS Tuna (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN). P 37 gave her position to HMS Tuna and asked her to leave P 37's area.

At 1900 A/18, HMS P 37 surfaced and set course for zone 'W' in accordance with orders from F.O.S. which were contained in his signal timed 1644 A/18. This signal had contained the following orders; Patrol in zones 'E' and 'W'. If weather conditions and other circumstances are favourable you should proceed as far as position 'Q' and then retire. Position 'Q' is 190° 17 miles from the position mentioned by HMS Taku in paragraph 4 of page 3 of section 44 part IV of the Submarine Intelligence Summary dated 14th August 1941. [We have been unable to locate this Submarine Intelligence Summary as of March 2026 but will try to find it in the future.] (9)
19 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1306 A/19, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); Remain in zone 'W'. (10)

19 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1452 A/19, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Tuna (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) and HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); Following movements are to take place after dark tonight; 1) Tuna to patrol in the vicinity of position 63°14'N, 07°07'E. 2) P 37 to patrol in the vicinity of position 63°22'N, 07°30'E.

In a signal timed 1741 A/19, F.O.S. informed the submarines of the following; This is to remind you that no undue risk should be taken in maintaining patrol, ordered in my l452A/19 and you must not hesitate to withdraw if you are being harassed. (10)
Object: destruction of enemy shipping off Tromso, Norway.

Around 0600 N/19, the battleship HMS King George V (Capt. W.R. Patterson, CB, CVO, RN, flying the flag of A/Admiral J.C. Tovey, KCB, KBE, DSO, RN, C-in-C Home Fleet), HMS Victorious (Capt. H.C. Bovell, CBE, RN), heavy cruiser HMS Berwick (Capt. G.H. Faulkner, DSC, RN) and the destroyers HMS Inglefield (Capt. P. Todd, DSO, RN), HMS Ashanti (Cdr. R.G. Onslow, RN), HMS Bedouin (Cdr. B.G. Scurfield, OBE, RN), HMS Eskimo (Cdr. E.G. Le Geyt, RN), HMS Icarus (Lt.Cdr. C.D. Maud, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. C.H. Campbell, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Echo (Lt.Cdr. N. Lanyon, RN) departed Hvalfjord.

Around 0845 Z/20, they were joined by the destroyers HMS Onslow (Capt. H.T. Armstong, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Punjabi (Lt.Cdr. J.M.G. Waldegrave, DSC, RN) and HMS Tartar (Cdr. R.T. White, DSO, RN) which came from Seidisfjord.
The destroyers HMS Ashanti, HMS Bedouin, HMS Eskimo and HMS Icarus were then detached to Seidisfjord to fuel. They returned around 1630 Z/20.
HMS Inglefield, HMS Fury and HMS Echo were then detached to Seidisfjord while the other ships proceeded on the operation.

At noon on 21 February a reconnaissance aircraft reported an enemy force of two battleships and three cruisers (this were actually the heavy cruisers Prinz Eugen, Admiral Scheer and the destroyers Z 4 / Richard Beitzen, Z 5 / Paul Jacobi, Z 7 / Hermann Schoemann, Z 14 / Friedrich Ihn, Z 25) off the south coast of Norway steering north. The Force assigned to operation EO then immediately changed course to the southward in the hope of reaching a position from which HMS Victorious could launch a night torpedo bomber attack. Operation EO was thus abandoned. Coastal and Bomber command aircraft set out to attack and submarines patrolling of the Norwegian coast (HMS Trident, HMS Tuna, HMS P 37 and FFS Minerve) concentrated in the southern approaches to Trondheim.

No further enemy reports were received, but the Fleet continued on a southerly course so that aircraft from HMS Victorious could be launched around 0300 hours on 22 February and sweep down the coast in the vicinity of Stadtlandet in the hope of sighting the enemy force.
Later that day an aircraft report was received showing that the enemy had retired and the operation was therefore postponed. The battlefleet then retired to the north-westward.

On 22 February a further report of the enemy proceeding northwards was received. The battlefleet then steamed south again and after dark closed the Norwegian coast. At 1800 A/22, HMS Victorious, HMS Berwick, HMS Bedouin, HMS Eskimo, HMS Punjabi and HMS Icarus were detached to proceed ahead.

The escort destroyers HMS Chiddingfold (Lt.Cdr. L.W.L. Argles, RN) and HMS Grove (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Rylands, RN) departed Scapa Flow around 1900 A/22 for air sea rescue duties for RAF aircraft operating against the German force.

At 0100 A/23, ten torpedo aircraft were flown off from HMS Victorious followed by seven more around 0145 A/23. All aircraft swept down the Norwegian coast in weather which was rapidly deteriorating. The enemy was not sighted, mainly to due to the very bad visibility. Following the sortie fourteen aircraft landed safely at Sumburgh, Shetland Islands. Three aircraft were lost.
Around 0600 A/23, the enemy was spotted again when the submarine HMS Trident attacked them and heavily damaged the Prinz Eugen.

HMS Victorious, HMS Berwick and their four escorting destroyers rejoined HMS King George V and her three escorting destroyers around 0900 A/23. Course was then set to Scapa Flow where they arrived around 1630 A/23. (13)

21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1231 A/21 the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following info to HMS Trident (Cdr. G.M. Sladen, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Tuna (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); At 1115 A/21, two battleships, three cruisers escorted by destroyers in position 55°48'N, 06°10'E, course 360°, estimated speed 23 to 25 knots.

In a signal timed 2054 A/21, F.O.S. sent the following orders to the submarines; Submarines are to lie well off the coast tonight and tomorrow, conserving batteries to the utmost. Submarines are to be in their patrol positions not later than 0100 A/23. Nothing smaller than cruisers is to be attacked.

In a signal timed 2213 A/21 the F.O.S. sent the following orders to the submarines; A) My 2054 A/21. Submarine patrol positions are to be as follows; P 37 63°22'N, 07°30'E. Tuna 63°14'N, 07°07'E. Trident 63°13'N, 06°54'E. Minerve 63°25'N, 07°22'E. B) If practicable P 37 and Tuna should close toward Great Inagua Isles light before dawn/23.

In a signal timed 2214 A/21, the F.O.S. sent the following orders to the submarines; I consider it improbable the enemy battle cruisers are present and air reconnaissance shows the enemy force has been much delayed. Torpedo depth sets are to be 12 feet except Trident's C.R.R. which are to be set to 20 feet. (10)

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1208 A/22, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders / information to HMS Trident (Cdr. G.M. Sladen, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Tuna (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); A) When it is certain that enemy main units have passed you either through having sighted them or through receipt of definite information from me, submarines are to withdraw to vicinity of following positions without further orders. Minerve 64°15'N, 07°20'E. P 37 63°52'N, 06°00'E. Tuna 63°23'N, 05°17'E. Trident 62°40'N, 05°10'E. B) It seems probable that P 37 and Tuna will be ordered to leave patrol after dark tomorrow.

In a signal timed 1612 A/22, they were informed of the following; One of you was probably sighted by a U-Boat at 1241 hours today. [U-435 arrived at Trondheim on that day but reported nothing. No other U-boat was in the area.]
In a signal timed 1947 A/22 the submarines were informed of the following; My 1231 A/21 and 2214 A/21. Main unit concerned consists of one pocket battleship and one 8" cruiser. (10)

23 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 0717 A/23, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following information to HMS Trident (Cdr. G.M. Sladen, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Tuna (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) and FFS Minerve (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); One pocket battleship, one cruiser and three destroyers in position 63°11'N, 07°01'E at 0602 A/23. Enemy course 070°.
In a signal timed 0759 A/23, F.O.S. informed the submarines of the following; My 0717 A/23. Unless damaged enemy may be assumed to have entered Gripholen.

In a signals timed 1019 A/23 the F.O.S. sent the following orders to FFS Minerve and HMS Trident; A) After dark/23, Minerve is to proceed to patrol in the vicinity of position 65°05'N, 10°00'E. B) After dark/24, Trident is to proceed to patrol in the vicinity of position 65°25'N, 08°40'E.

In signals timed 1115 A/23 and 1923 A/23 the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to HMS Tuna and HMS P 37. 1) Tuna is to leave patrol tonight passing through following positions to rendezvous with escort in position 090° - Bard Head (Shetland Islands) - 5 miles at 1200 A/25 for onward passage to Holy Loch. 2) P 37 leaves patrol tonight passing through following positions to rendezvous with escort in position 090° - Bard Head - 5 miles at 1200 A/26 for onward passage to Dundee. Bombing restrictions have been arranged.

In a signal timed 1827 A/23, the F.O.S. sent the following orders to FFS Minerve and HMS Trident; My 1019 A/23. Paragraph A. Amend Minerve's patrol position to 65°05'N, 09°45'E. Cancel paragraph B and substitute. After dark today Trident is to proceed to patrol vicinity of the line joining positions 62°35'N, 05°00' and 62°54'N, 05°38'E. (10)
23 Feb 1942 In the evening, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) left patrol to proceed to Dundee in accordance with the Capt. S.9's signal timed 1115 A/23. (11)
27 Feb 1942 At 1700 A/27, off Bard Head, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN), made contact with her escort towards Dundee, HMS Stella Pegasi (T/Lt. F.S. Howell, RNR). (11)
28 Feb 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Dundee. (11)
5 Mar 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) developed problems with her battery which required her to be taken in hand for repairs. (8)
25 Mar 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Dundee for Lerwick. She was escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (8)
26 Mar 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived at Lerwick from Dundee. (8)
27 Mar 1942 At 0700 A/27, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Lerwick for her 3rd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off the Norwegian coast to the north of Trondheim.

She is routed as follows; A) 090° - Out Skerries 5 miles. B) 61°27'N, 00°32'W. C) 62°22'N, 04°25'E. D) 64°30 N, 08°35'E, thence patrol on a line between the Kya lighthouse and position 64°21'N, 10°00'E. P 37 was to proceed on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion when west of meridian of Greenwich, thence diving by day.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (11)

27 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 0927 A/27, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); Delete paragraph 4 (B) of your patrol orders and substitute submerge 25 miles either side line joining 61°40'N, 00°35'E and 62°18'N, 04°00'E from 1900 A/27 until further notice. (14)

28 Mar 1942 At 0200 A/28, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines signal timed 2314 A/27 which contained the following orders; Paragraph 'D' of your patrol orders. Amend to read P 37 vicinity of 62°16'N, 02°55'E, probably on the surface through position 'T' (63°28'N, 06°30'E). to patrol in 'K 7'. (15)

28 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1539 A/28, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); From Position 'T' (63°28'N, 06°30'E), P 37 is to proceed diving by day through position 'U' (64°26'N, 08°50'E) to patrol in the vicinity of position 64°22'N, 09°37'E moving after dark 30th to relieve HMS Tuna (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) in position 64°30'N, 10°24'E. (14)

30 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1834 A/30, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); You are to proceed after dark tonight from approximate position 64°22'N, 09°37'E to the vicinity of position 64°36'N, 08°25'E.
P 37 was also ordered to retain a full salvo of torpedoes for use against enemy heavy units. (14)

1 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2216 A/1, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); Proceed forthwith to patrol during daylight between the Kya lighthouse and position 64°32'N, 10°35'E, keeping as near as practicable to position 64°30'N, 10°25'E. Withdraw as necessary to charge. (16)

2 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1604 A/2, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); My 2216 A/1. Withdraw at descretion but be in position by dawn/4. (16)

4 Apr 1942 At 0400 A/4, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) sighted the Kya lighthouse. She then commenced to close position 64°30'N, 10°35'E near which she patrolled from 4 to 11 April 1942 roughly between 0600 and 2000 hours each day except for 8 April 1942 when the patrol position was not closed until 0900 A/8 due to the poor visibility. A big number of ships were seen during this period but no attacks were made as only enemy vessels of cruisers and above might be attacked.
At 0630 A/4, a three-island northbound tramp ship of around 1500 to 2000 tons was sighted at a range of 8000 yards.
At 1200 A/4, an escorted southbound tanker of about 7000 to 8000 tons was sighted. The vessel was armed. Range was 6000 yards.

This was most probably the German tanker Orion (former Norwegian, 8064 GRT, built 1936) which had departed Sandnessjøen at 0200 B/3 escorted by the auxiliary patrol vessel NSa 01 / Friedrich Wilhelm zu Pferde. [It is doubful that this escort vessel was still with the tanker, most likely she would have been relieved by this time.] This tanker passed Kristiansund (Nord) around 1000 B/5 but now escorted by the auxiliary patrol vessel NM 24.
At 1245 A/4, three-island northbound tramp ship of around 1500 tons was sighted at a range of 6000 yards.
At 1430 A/4, a modern 3000 tons northbound cargo vessel was sighted at a range of 6000 yards. The vessel was armed.
At 1815 A/4, a small southbound coastal packet boat of about 1000 tons was sighted at a range of 5000 yards.
[We have been unable to positively identify the other shipping sighted.] (17)
5 Apr 1942 At 0840 B/5, while patrolling near position 64°30'N, 10°35'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN), sighted two small southbound coasters of about 200 tons each at a range of about 6000 yards.
At 0900 B/5, HMS P 37 sighted a southbound three-island tramp of about 1500 tons at a range of 6000 yards.
At 1000 B/5, a southbound modern cargo vessel of about 4000 tons was sighted at a range of 5000 yards.
At 1005 B/5, a southbound three-island tramp of about 1500 tons at a range of 5000 yards.
At 1045 B/5, a small merchant vessel was seen at a range of 6 miles. This vessel was leaving Buholmrasa and then proceeded southwards.
At 1150 B/5, a small coastal packet boat of about 600 tons was seen at a range of 6000 yards. This vessel was northbound.
At 1200 B/5, what are thought to be enemy patrol vessels were seen to the north, near Villa. They disappeared from sight soon in the rather poor visibility.
At 1230 B/5, an escorted southbound three-island tramp of about 3000 tons at a range of 5000 yards.
At 2300 B/5, two vessels were sighted which were patrolling a southerly direction. Range was 2 miles.
As only enemy vessels of cruisers and above might be attacked, HMS P 37 had to let all these vessels pass.
[We have been unable to positively identify any of the shipping sighted on this day.] (11)

6 Apr 1942 At 0645 B/6, while patrolling near position 64°30'N, 10°35'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN), sighted a large destroyer leaving Buholmrasa. Range was 4 miles. Enemy course 330°, speed 24 knots. The range was closed to 4500 yards. The enemy later changed course to 020° and disappeared in the direction of Rorvik.
This was the German destroyer Z 7 / Hermann Shoemann.
At 0745 B/6, an armed trawler was seen entering Buholmrasa from the north.
At 0800 B/6, a southbound modern cargo vessel of about 5000 tons was sighted at a range of 6000 yards.
At 0915 B/6, a southbound laden three-island tramp of about 2500 tons with a gun mounted forward was seen at a range of 6000 yards.

Also a vessel, suspected to be an enemy patrol vessel, was seen to the north proceeding to the south-west. Range was 5 miles and eventually this vessel passed at a range of 3 miles proceeding in the direction of Kya. It eventually disappeared behind the lighthouse.
At 1300 B/6, a southbound laden three-island tramp of about 3000 to 4000 tons was seen at a range of 8000 yards.
At 1330 B/6, a northbound auxiliary schooner of about 500 tons was seen at a range of 7000 yards and also a northbound sailing cutter at a range of 8000 yards.
At 1345 B/6, a southbound three-island tramp of about 2000 tons was seen at a range of 8000 yards.

At 1500 B/6, the vessels first seen at 0915 A/6 and suspected to be an enemy patrol vessel was seen to the south-west near the Kya lighthouse. The vessel was proceeding to the north-east at a range of 6 to 7 miles. P 37 was on his track so she ran out at full speed. When next seen the enemy patrol vessel was still coming straight towards at a range of 3.5 miles. The enemy vessel was closing fast but stopping at intervals, apparently to listen. Lt. Winter considered P 37 had been heard so all unnecessary motors were stopped. The enemy, lost touch and commenced sweeping at 5 knots on mean course 050°. HE of the enemy was inaudible at his speed of 5 knots. The enemy vessel eventually passed at a range of 3500 yards and subsequently disappeared to the north.
At 1800 B/6, an armed trawler was seen entering Buholmrasa coming from the north. Enemy speed was 12 knots. Range was 3000 yards.
[We have been unable to positively identify the other shipping sighted.] (18)

7 Apr 1942 At 0645 B/7, while patrolling near position 64°30'N, 10°35'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN), sighted two patrol vessels close to Villa steering south-west while sweeping. { 37 took avoiding action but the vessels turned to the north-east when the range was still 5 miles.
At 0745 B/7, a small southbound coaster of about 500 tons was sighted at a range of 8000 yards.
At 0800 B/7, a small 500 tons coaster was seen proceeding southwards. Range was 6000 yards.
At 1140 B/7, a three-island coaster of about 1000 to 1500 tons was seen at a range of 7000 yards. The vessel was southbound.
At 1200 B/7, a three island coaster of about 1000 to 1500 tons was seen at a range of 7000 yards. This vessel was also southbound.
At 1330 B/7, a large laden tanker of about 10000 to 11000 tons was seen at a range of 7000 yards. The vessel was unescorted and northbound at a speed of 12 knots.
This was most likely the German tanker Forbach (Former Dutch Perna, 7984 GRT, built 1935) which had departed Trondheim northbound at 0330 B/7.
At 1420 B/7, a 500 tons southbound coaster was seen at a range of 6000 yards.
At 1500 B/7, a large three-island cargo ship of about 5000 tons was seen at a range of 7000 yards. The vessel was northbound.

At 1900 B/7, two patrol vessels were seen approaching from the direction of Kya proceeding on course 050° evidently sweeping. Avoiding action was taken. The nearest patrol vessel passed at a range of 6000 yards. The enemy vessels eventually disappeared to the north.
[We have been unable to positively identify the other shipping sighted.] (19)

8 Apr 1942 At 1330 A/8, while patrolling near position 64°30'N, 10°35'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN), two patrol vessels close to Villa steering to the south-west while sweeping. Avoiding action was taken by turning to the north-west. The nearest patrol vessel passed at a range of 4000 yards and the enemy vessels eventually disappeared beyond Kya. After the patrol vessels had passed P 37 set course to return to the patrol position.
At 1345 B/8, a medium sized cargo ship of about 3000 tons was seen at a range of 5000 yards. The vessel was southbound.
At 1400 B/8, a three-island coaster of about 1500 tons was sighted at a range of 5 miles. The vessel was seen to leave Buholmrasa for the south.
At 1600 B/8, a camouflaged escort vessel was seen to leave Buholmrasa for the north at a speed of 12 knots. This vessel was proceeding northwards inside Grunnane.

At 1930 B/8, a patrol vessel was seen near Kya proceeding to the north-east. This vessel was to pass close to P 37 ran westwards taking precautions to not be heard. The patrol passed at a range of 3500 yards and disappeared to the north.
[We have been unable to positively identify the shipping sighted.] (11)
9 Apr 1942 At 0800 B/9, while patrolling near position 64°30'N, 10°35'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN), sighted a northbound three-island coaster of about 1000 tons at a range of 6000 yards.
At 0900 B/9, a coastal mail packet boat of about 1000 tons was sighted at a range of 6000 yards. The vessel was northbound.
At 1200 B/9, two patrol vessels were seen near Villa proceeding to the south-west while sweeping. Avoiding action was taken but the vessels turned to the north-east when the range was 6000 yards.
At 1330 B/9, a laden three-island coaster of about 1500 tons was sighted at a range of 5000 yards. The vessel was southbound.
10 Apr 1942 At 0800 B/10, while patrolling near position 64°30'N, 10°35'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN), sighted patrol vessels to the north-east. Shortly afterwards a cargo ship was seen.

At 0830 B/10, a southbound three-island cargo ship of about 5000 tons was seen at a range of 3000 yards. The vessel was seen to be armed with a gun on the forecastle. A dummy attack was carried out on this ship.
A patrol vessel passed at a range of 2500 yards. The patrol vessels later proceeded in the direction of Kya but when abreast Kya they were observed to proceed towards Buholmrasa at 15 knots.
At 1600 B/10, a patrol vessel was seen to leave Buholmrasa proceeding on course 270° at 12 knots.

At 1615 B/10, a second patrol vessel was seen leaving Buholmrasa and coming straight towards at 12 knots. Shortly afterwards this vessel slowed to 5 knots and commenced sweeping on course 030°. P 37 took avoiding action. The nearest patrol vessel passed at a range of 2500 yards. No HE of this vessel was picked up.

10 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1413 B/10, The Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); Leave patrol after dark/11 diving by day routed as follows. A) 64°54'N. 09°20'E. B) 62°47'N, 03°26’E. C) 61°34'N, 00°28'W thence on surface at Commanding Officers discretion through position D) 091° - Out Skerries - 6 miles. E) 089° - Bard Head - 5 miles to Lerwick. Make rendezvous with the escort to Lerwick in position 'E' at 1400 B/15. Bombing restrictions in force until further notice. [Details not included in the signal in the war diary summaries.] You may meet FFS Rubis (Lt. H.L.G. Rousselot) in the vicinity of position 'C'. (16)
11 Apr 1942 At 1930 B/11, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) left patrol to return to Lerwick. (11)

14 Apr 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) fired two torpedoes at a suspected U-boat in the Norwegian Sea. Two torpedoes were fired and one torpedo explosion was heard. No U-boats were operating near this position so the contact must have been bogus, perhaps the contact was a whale or a shoal of porpoises.
1015 B/14 – In approximate position 62°42’N, 03°10’E while proceeding dived heard strange noises. Suspected a U-boat. Closed to investigate.
During the next period the suspected U-boat was tracked.
1353 B/14 – Fired two torpedoes against suspected U-boat.
1403 B/14 – Heard a loud explosion.
1420 B/14 – Closed the position of the explosion, found nothing.
1600 B/14 - Proceeded. (11)
16 Apr 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Lerwick.
She departed for Dundee later the same day (around 1500 B/16) together with FFS Rubis (Lt. H.L.G. Rousselot). They were escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (11)
17 Apr 1942 Around 1830 B/17, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived at Dundee from Lerwick.
She arrived at Dundee with several defects which required her to be docked. The date of her docking is (for the moment) unknown to us. (8)
1 May 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) is undocked. (8)
14 May 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Dundee for Lerwick. She was escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (8)
15 May 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived at Lerwick from Dundee. (8)

16 May 1942 At 1300 B/16, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Lerwick for her 4th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off northern Norway to provide cover during convoy operations to and from northern Russia (convoy PQ 16 / QP 12).
P 37 was routed as follows; 088° - Out Skerries - 6 miles, 69°40'N, 08°35'E to zone 'K 31'.

Half an hour after departure the signal timed 1252 B/16 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) was received which contained the following orders; Proceed on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion at best speed through position 61°01'N, 00°02'W to patrol in the vicinity of position 61°50'N, 04°02'E. Total bombing restrictions arranged.
At 2300 B/16, F.O.S. signal timed 2106 B/16 was received which contained the following orders; My 1252 B/16. Proceed to position 'N' (61°20'N, 04°05'E).
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (20)
17 May 1942 At 0950 B/17, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) dived in position 'N' (61°20'N, 04°05'E).
At 1203 B/17, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 0958 B/17 was received which ordered her to patrol in position 'O' (62° 01'N, 04°07'E). (20)

18 May 1942 At 2045 B/18, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 1855 B/18 which had contained the following orders; You are to leave patrol tonight proceeding well clear position 'O' and then on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion as follows; Leave patrol at 2359 B/18 through positions 'A' (63°40'N., 02°20'E), 'B' (70°01'N, 09°05'E to zone 'K 31'. (20)

22 May 1942 At 0630 B/22, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived in the centre of the north-west half of zone 'K 31' and commenced to carry out a surface patrol within 6 miles of this position until 0800 A/26. (11)

25 May 1942 In signals timed 1628 B/25 and 1712 B/25, the Flag Officer Submarines sent the following orders to HrMs O 10 (Lt. Baron D.T. Mackay, RNN), HMS P 614 (Lt. D.J. Beckley, RN), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. R.Q. Røren), HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); At 0800 B/26, O 10, P 614, Uredd, P 37 and P 46 proceed so as to be in zones 'K 26', 'K 28', 'K 30', 'K 32' and 'K 34' respectively by 2000 B/26. (21)

26 May 1942 At 2000 B/26, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived in the mid position of the north-west half of zone 'K 32' where a diving patrol was kept. Between 2000 B/26 and 0230 B/28 she remained within 6 miles of the patrol position.
At 2140 B/26, distant explosions were heard.

27 May 1942 In signals timed 1136 B/27, the Flag Officer Submarine sent the following orders to HrMs O 10 (Lt. Baron D.T. Mackay, RNN), HMS P 614 (Lt. D.J. Beckley, RN), HNoMS Uredd (Lt. R.Q. Røren), HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); At 0600 B/28, O 10, P 614, Uredd, P 37 and P 46 are to leave their patrol zones and return to Lerwick as will be ordered by the Capt. S.9.

In a signal timed 1741 B/9, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to the submarines; Leave patrol at 0600 B/28 for position 083° - Out Skerries - 6 miles, thence Lerwick routed as follows. 1) O 10 and P 614 direct. 2) Uredd through position 70°02'N, 08°25'E. 3) P 37 through position 69°24'N, 08°30'E. 4) P 46 through position 69°15'N, 08°10'E. The submarines are to proceed on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion and are to adjust speed so as to arrive at Lerwick as follows; O 10 1500 B/31. P 614 2000 B/31. Uredd 0400 B/1 P 37 1100 B/1. P 46 1800 B/1. (21)
1 Jun 1942 At 0044 B/1, in position 60°42'N, 00°30'W, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 240° at a range of 3 to 4 miles.
At 0047 B/1, the submarine was identified as being HrMs O 10 (Lt. Baron D.T. Mackay, RNN) who thus was late.

At 0500 B/1, the escort for HrMs O 10 was sighted off Bard Head at a range of 5 miles. HMS P 37 increased speed so as also make rendezvous and arrive at Lerwick with HrMs O 10 and the escort which turned out to be HMS Harmattan (T/Lt. J.E. Polmont, RNVR).
Around 0600 B/1, HMS P 37 ended her 4th war patrol at Lerwick.

She departed later the same day for Dundee. Passage was made together with HNoMS Uredd (Lt. R.Q. Rören). They were escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). Off Scapa Flow they were joined by HrMs O 14 (Lt.Cdr. H.A.W. Goossens, RNN) that also was to proceed to Dundee. (11)
2 Jun 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) arrived at Dundee from Lerwick. (8)
4 Jun 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) is docked at Dundee. (8)
17 Jun 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) is undocked. (8)
22 Jun 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) is briefly docked at Dundee. She is undocked later the same day. (22)

29 Jun 1942 Around 1400 B/29, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Dundee for Gibraltar. Until the Clyde area passage was made together with HrMs O 24 (Lt. W.J. de Vries, RNN) (which joined at 1930 B/29) and escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR) until position 55°09'N, 05°46'W when, at 0430 B/2, HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN) took over the escort of HMS P 37 and the other two ships proceeded up the Clyde.
P 37 then proceeded south through the Irish Sea escorted by HMS White Bear until 0130 B/4 when they parted company in position 290° - Bishops Rock - 5 miles.
From Biships Rock, HMS P 37 was routed as follows; A) 49°10'N, 12°20'W. B) 43°02'N, 12°20'W. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.).
P 37 was to proceed to Malta where she was to join the 10th submarine flotilla. (11)
29 Jun 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 37 during the passage from Dundee to Gibraltar see the map below.

1 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 1234 B/1, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); Part company with the escort at 0030 B/4 in position 350° - Bishop Rock - 10 miles. Further routing as follows; 50°00'N, 10°00'W. 49°35'N, 12°55'W. 43°02'N, 12°20'W at 0030 B/10. (23)

5 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 1608 B/5, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN); Onward route on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through the following positions; 37°01'N, 09°59'W at 2100 B/11. 225° - Cape St.Vincent - 20 miles at 0145 B/12. Thence diving by day on the 12th to make rendezvous with escort in position 350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles at 0930 B/13, thence onward to Gibraltar. Bombing restrictions arranged. If unable to comply within two hours report. (23)

8 Jul 1942 At 1745 B/8, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) received the Flag Officers Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 1637 B/8 which had contained the following orders; Proceed forthwith on surface at your discretion to assist aircraft dinghy in position 45°22'N, 07°01'E. Headquarters Coastal Command is requested to arrange total bombing restrictions 35 miles either side of line joining 45°20'N, 12°30' and above position forthwith until further notice.
At 1800 B/8, HMS P 37 altered course to comply with these orders.

At 1925 B/8, the F.O.S. signal timed 1839 B/8 was received in which the orders in the signal timed 1637 B/8 were cancelled and HMS P 37 was to proceed in accordance with the previous orders. P 37 altered course once more altered course to comply with these orders. (24)

12 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 1738 B/12, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) of the following; HMS Arctic Ranger and HMRT Salvonia will sail from Gibraltar at 1730 B/12 for position 33°30'N, 22°01'W at 10 knots. (25)
13 Jul 1942 At 0920 B/13, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) made rendezvous with the escort [not identified] in position 350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles.
At 1300 B/13, HMS P 37 arrived at Gibraltar. She had arrived with defects to her battery for which she was taken in hand for repairs. (11)
28 Jul 1942 At 1715 B/28, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 5th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Alboran Sea in area 'PP'.
For the daily positions of HMS P 37 during this patrol see the map below.

29 Jul 1942 At 0110 B/29, in position 36°27'N, 04°43'W, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) passed an illuminated tanker of approximately 1500 tons proceeding on a course of approximately 270°. A dummy attack was carried out on this vessel which most likely was Spanish.
At 0600 B/29, HMS P 37 dived in position 36°39'N, 04°00'W and patrolled in the northern sector of her patrol area between longitudes 04°00'W and 03°50'W.
At 1015 B/29, a dummy attack was carried out on a small tanker. This was the only ship seen while dived on this day except for some small fishing vessels.

At 1730 B/29, the signal timed 1730 B/29 from the Vice-Admiral Commanding North Atlantic was received. P 37 set course of the north-west corner of her patrol area in accordance with the orders received. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]

At 2220 B/29, HMS P 37 surfaced to carry out an exercises attack and at 2223 B/29 No.1 and No.2 torpedo tubes were fired in error. Heavy under explosions were heard shortly afterwards and an illuminated steam ship 3 miles away proceeded on her way.

At 2300 B/29, HMS P 37 moved to the westward to the rendezvous position in 086° - Europa Point - 8 miles keeping 3 to 4 miles off the Spanish Coast. This in accordance with the Vice-Admiral Commanding North Atlantic's signal timed 1338 B/29 which had contained the following orders for HMS P 37; You are to leave patrol at 0001 B/30, through position 36°39'N, 04°10'W and proceed 3.5 miles off the coast of Spain through position 36°20'N, 05°08' thence direct to position 086° - Europa Point Lighthouse - 8 miles to make rendezvous with HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) at 0900 B/30. Either destroyer or motor launch on 'U' Patrol will transfer the Commander Submarines with exercise orders from P 46 to P 37. Total bombing restrictions have been arranged accordingly. (26)

30 Jul 1942 At 0900 B/30, HMS P 37 (Lt. H. Winter, RN) met HMS Vidette (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Walmsley, DSC, RN) which transferred the Commander Submarines after which exercises commenced during which practice attacks were made on HMS Vidette. At 1500 B/30, HMS P 37 ended her 5th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (11)
3 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Gibraltar. (27)

5 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted attack exercises off Gibraltar during which HMS Jonquil (Lt.Cdr. R.E.H. Partington, RD, RNR) acted as target. HMS P 37 also carried out gunnery exercises. (27)
6 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Gibraltar together with HMS Jonquil (Lt.Cdr. R.E.H. Partington, RD, RNR). (27)

7 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Gibraltar together with HMS Black Swan (Cdr. T.A.C. Pakenham, RN) and HMS Carnation (Lt. A. Branson, RNR). On completion of the A/S exercises a practice attack was made on HMS Carnation. (27)
9 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. (27)
11 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. (27)
15 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) is docked in No.3 graving dock at Gibraltar. (27)
16 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) is undocked. (27)
19 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. (27)
21 Aug 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. (27)
1 Sep 1942 Upon departure from Gibraltar at 0800 A/1, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Gibraltar together with HMS Charybdis (Capt. G.A.W. Voelcker, RN).
On completion of the exercises HMS P 37 departed for her 6th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Alboran Sea near Melilla.
[No patrol report is available for this patrol.] (11)
1 Sep 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 37 during her 6th war patrol see the map below.

4 Sep 1942 In a signal timed the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); Leave patrol to pass position 'G' at 2030 A/5 thence on surface at your discretion direct to arrive at Gibraltar at 0930 A/6. Do not cross 05°00'W before 0730 A/6. Bombing restrictions arranged accordingly. (28)

5 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1810 A/5, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) of the following; A Sunderland reported at 1725 A/5, a U-boat in position 36°31'N, 03°00'W, course 260°, speed 8 knots.
[No enemy U-boat was in the area though.]
In a signal timed 1840 A/5, F.O.C.N.A. ordered HMS P 37 to remain in her patrol area until further orders. The receipt of this signal was to be acknowledged. (28)

6 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1136 A/6, the Flag Officer Submarines North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders / information to HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); My 1840 A/5. Leave patrol pass position 'G' at 0130 A/7 and proceeding on surface direct to arrive at Gibraltar at 1330 A/7. Swordfish aircraft will carry out A.S.V sweeps for U-Boats within 25 miles of Almina Pt. from 2030 A/6 until 0700 A/7. You are not to approach within 10 miles of this area before 0700 A/7. HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN) pass Europa Point at 1600 A/6 on surface on course 81°, speed 12 knots. (28)

6 Sep 1942 At 2330 A/6, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Gibraltar. A few ships had been seen during the patrol but all were most likely Spanish except for a British convoy to Melilla on which a practice attack had been made. (29)
7 Sep 1942 At 1210 A/7, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (11)
10 Sep 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) conducted D/G trials at Gibraltar. (29)

13 Sep 1942 At 1600 A/13, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 7th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was to proceed to Malta and to make a short patrol off Marettimo Island.
She was to pass through the following positions; 'M' (35°45'N, 02°52'W). 'N' (36°54'N, 01°25'E). 'O' (38°05'N, 08°59'E) at 0130 A/20. Further onward routing would be signalled by the Capt. S.10.
[No patrol report for this patrol could be found in the National Archives at London.]

15 Sep 1942 At 0720 A/15, in approximate position 36°12'N, 00°50'W, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sighted a ship bearing 070° at a range of 7 miles. Course of this ship, which was Vichy-French, was 190°. (29)

19 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1947 B/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); Remain north and west of Marettimo. If you see an unescorted worth while target attack it at your discretion. Your passage through Q.B.B. 255 is delayed two or three days.

[This delay was caused by the failure of HMS Talisman (Lt.Cdr. M. Willmott, DSO, RN) to arrive at Malta as expected on the 18th. It was feared the route through the Italian minefields [Q.B.B. 255] had been compromised.] (28)
23 Sep 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) made the submerged eastbound passage of Q.B.B. 255 during this day. (29)
25 Sep 1942 Around 1350 B/25, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 1035 B/25. (29)

5 Oct 1942 At 1345 A/5, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol south of latitude 34°20'N, and west of longitude 12°30'E.
She was escorted out until 1610 A/5 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) following which HMS P 37 made a practice attack on the minesweeper before proceeding on patrol. (30)
5 Oct 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 37 during her 8th war patrol see the map below.
6 Oct 1942 At 1123 A/6, in position 34°37'N, 12°50'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) heard HE bearing 180°.
At 1135 A/6, contact was lost when the HE faded. It was thought the HE had been of an another submarine.
[No other submarine was in this area as far as we could determine.] (30)
7 Oct 1942 At 1230 A/7, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol to the south-east of Ras Turgoeness lighthouse. (11)

8 Oct 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sank the small Italian merchant vessel Lupa (349 GRT, built 1919) with gunfire east of Djerba, Tunisia in position 33°41'N, 11°44'E. HMS P 37 had first fired two torpedoes but these missed. Of the crew of the Lupa, two were wounded and the survivors reached Zarzis in a lifeboat.
She had sailed from Tunis at 0200 B/6 at 5.5 knots for Tripoli. She had previously sailed from Tripoli at 1800 B/23 (September) and reached Tunis at 2115 B/26 (September).
1433 A/8 - Sighted a small coaster on a southerly course. Closed to investigate.
1520 A/8 - In position 33°42'N, 11°14'E fired two torpedoes from 800 yards. The torpedoes are thought to have run under.
1530 A/8 – Engaged with the 12-pdr gun. Fired 40 rounds of H.E.
1542 A/8 - Placed a boarding party onboard to search for secret documents and demolish the ship.
1545 A/8 - Withdrew the boarding party. No documents of interest were found. The demolition charge was ignited with a 8 minute fuse. The ship had a cargo of wine and food.
1548 A/8 - Dived and withdrew from the area into deeper water.
1915 A/8 - Set course to patrol to the north-east of Ras Turgoeness lighthouse. (11)

9 Oct 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sank the Tunisian schooner Yasmina (by setting it on fire) and torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Alga (1851 GRT, built 1917) near Djerba, Tunisia in position 34°02'N, 11°05'E.
Yasmina had sailed from Sfax carrying 15 tons of foodstuffs and 10 tons of lime for Adjim. The crew of five reached the shore, and there were no casualties.

Alga had sailed from Palermo at 0300 B/7 via Trapani to Tripoli. An Italian seaplane located two wounded survivors and landed on the sea to rescue them. At 0945 B/10, the Italian air rescue ship Laurana (391 GRT, built 1939) sailed from Tripoli to search for survivors and picked up five more of them. She arrived at Zuara at 0430 B/11. An eighth survivor was found by the Tunisian schooner Julia at 1530 B/10. 44 were killed or missing.
1450 A/9 - Sighted a schooner on a southerly course. Closed.

1500 A/9 to 1600 A/9 - Sighted 5 more schooners spread over about 5 nautical miles. One pair kept consistently together and one of them had a crowsnest on the foremast. In view of them being A/S schooners and the number of aircraft in the vicinity, it was decided not to attack until dark.

1858 A/9 - In position 34°09'N, 11°06'E engaged the schooner that was closest to the shore with the 12pdr gun. Fired four rounds of High Explosive. The schooner was abandoned. Proceeded alongside and set fire to the schooner. The schooner was about 150 feet long and loaded with large sacks whose contents were not examined.
1910 A/9 - Began a search for other schooners.
2205 A/9 - Sighted a darkened southbound merchant ship of about 2500 tons.

2225 A/9 - In position 34°02'N, 11°05'E fired two torpedoes from 1500 yards. One hit was obtained and the ship blew up. HE was heard for 10 minutes, possibly an escort but none was seen. P 37 then retired to seaward to reload. (11)

10 Oct 1942 At 2220 A/10, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2015 A/10 in which she was ordered to keep in the north-east corner of her area for 48 hours. Course was set to comply with this order. (11)
11 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0030 A/11, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) informed the Capt. S.10 that she was short of ammunition for her deck gun.

In a signal timed 1027 A/11, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37; Proceed forthwith through position 35°01'N, 12°21'E to operate in area 'H' west of longitude given (12°21'E) between 35°01'N, and 36°21'E. (31)
12 Oct 1942 At 0500 A/12, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol to the south-east of Kuriat Island. (11)
14 Oct 1942 At 2235 A/14, south of Kuriat Island, in position 35°42'N, 11°00'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sighted three southbound lighted ships. She closed to attack.
At 2238 A/14, HE was heard to the eastward and the attack on the lighted ships, which were thought to be most likely Vichy-French, was broken off and course was altered to the east.
At 2242 A/14, the source of the HE was not in sight but from bearings of the HE the enemy was northbound so the attack was shifted again to the lighted ships.

These consisted of a small ship, probably a destroyer or torpedo boat which was also burning navigation lights, and two fairly large transports brilliantly illuminated. They were inside territorial waters. It was not possible to get inside 4000 yards or close enough to verify what the markings on their sides were. It seemed possible these were hospital ships though and torpedoes were therefore not fired and the attack was broken off at 2340 A/14.

Possibly the convoy sighted was the one made up of the Vichy French tranports Rabelais (4999 GRT, built 1921) and Oasis (727 GRT, built 1938) escorted by the minesweeping sloop La Batailleuse. This convoy was expected of Kerkennah Buoy No.2 at 0800 A/25. [See file DEFE3/852/p. 857]. (11)

15 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1129 A/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); Leave patrol as necessary through positions 35°19'N, 12°21'E and 35°31'N, 14°11'E to arrived at the end of the searched channel at 1015 A/18. (31)
16 Oct 1942 At 1930 A/16, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1129 A/15. (11)

17 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1251 A/17, Capt. S.10 sent the following submarines HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) the following orders; Previous orders for Utmost, P 37, P 42 and P 44 are cancelled. Proceed to be in following positions by dawn on the 19th. HMS Utmost to position 36°16'N, 11°58'E proceeding through position 36°47'N, 13°10'E en-route. HMS P 37 to position 35°45'N, 11°58'E proceeding through position 35°40'N, 12°54'E en-route. HMS P 42 to position 35°25'N, 12°21'E proceeding through position 33°20'N, 13°55'E en-route. HMS P 44 to position 35°12'N, 12°33'E proceeding through position 33°20'N, 14°35'E en-route. HMS P 44 if unable comply proceed through position 33°41'N, 13°03'E and steer 343° keeping south of 35°10'N. (32)
18 Oct 1942 At 0500 A/18, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) arrived in her ordered patrol position (35°45'N, 11°58'E).
At 2015 A/18, the first enemy report on the approaching convoy was received. (The Capt. S.10's signal timed 1736 A/18.) (11)

18 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1017 A/18, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) of the following; HMS Utmost was to move her assigned patrol position 10 miles to the south. HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) leaves the Malta searched channel sunset 18th direct for position 36°18'N, 11°53'E.

In a signal timed 1736 A/18, Capt. S.10 informed the above submarines that an aircraft had repored a large tanker and three merchant ships escorted by seven destroyers in position 38°15'N, 12°39'E, course 200°, speed 8 knots at 1615 A/18.

The 'DELTA' convoy was made up of the transports Titania (Italian, 5397 GRT, built 1918), Beppe (Italian, 4859 GRT, built 1912) and Capo Orso (Italian, 3149 GRT, built 1916), screened by the destroyers Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani sailed from Naples at 1526-1530 B/17. The destroyers Giovanni da Verazzano and Antonio Pigafetta (escort leader) sailed from Naples at 1730 B/17 and 1905 B/17 respectively and joined the convoy at 2040 B/17.

The tanker Saturno (Italian, 5022 GRT, built 1914) sailed from Cagliari at 1535-1600 B/17 escorted by the torpedo boat Nicola Fabrizi. The destroyers Antonio da Noli and AscariSaturno. At 0655 B/18, Antonio da Noli joined the convoy and two hours later so did the Ascari.

At 1212 B/18-1235 B/18, the Titania and Saturno sections merged at 1212 B/18-1235 B/18. The torpedo boat Centauro sailed from Trapani at 1910 B/18 to relieve Fabrizi, which went on to this harbour, arriving at 2040 B/18.

The torpedo boat Sagittario had sailed from Palermo at 2015 B/17 to do an A/S sweep off Pantelleria. At 0028 B/19, she was ordered by Marina Messina to join the convoy off Pantelleria, to replace the torpedo boat Centauro. She met the convoy at 0923 B/19 and took position astern after which the Centauro left the convoy.

At 1250 B/19, Beppe and Giovanni da Verazzano were torpedoed and sunk by HMS P 37. At the time of the attack, the convoy was formed of Beppe, Capo Orso, Titania and Saturno, screened by Vincenzo Gioberti, Antonio da Noli, Antonio Pigafetta, Alfredo Oriani, Giovanni da Verazzano, Ascari and Sagittario. Oriani and Gioberti were ordered to hunt the submarine. They dropped depth charges and then picked up the survivors. The two destroyers proceeded to Lampedusa where they transferred the survivors to a fishing vessel which came out of the port; they then rejoined the convoy at 2006 B/19.

At 1610 A/19, HMS P 42 attacked the convoy with four torpedoes but no hits were obtained. Following this failed attack, HMS P 42 was depth charged and sustained damage which eventually forced her to abandon her war patrol.

At 2330 B/19, Titania was hit by a torpedo bomber in position 34°45'N, 12°31'E. Alfredo Oriani, Ascari and Sagittario were detached to assist the stricken vessel. Sagittario took some seriously wounded on board and brought them to Trapani at 0725 B/20. Titania was finished off by HMS P 211 at 0720 B/20. The two destroyers picked up the survivors and were ordered to land them at Tripoli if their autonomy allowed it or turn back to Trapani. Alfredo Oriani continued to Tripoli, which was reached at 1436 B/20. Ascari turned back and reached Trapani at 2251 B/20.
At 0230 B/20, the torpedo boat Circe departed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy.
Saturno, Capo Orso, Antonio Pigafetta, Antonio da Noli, Vincenzo Gioberti and Circe arrived at Tripoli at 1206 B/20-1420 B/20.

In a signal timed 2349 A/18, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the five submarines listed above; After passage of convoy on 19th submariens are to proceed as follows to arrive end of searched channel 1031 A on dates given: P 211 direct to arrive 20/10. Utmost through positions 35°46'N, 12°56'E and 35°40'N, 14°06'E and P 37 direct to position 35°32'N, 14°12'E both to arrive 21/10. P 42 and P 44 to spend daylight hours 20/10 vicinity Kuriat and position 35°24'N, 12°24'E respectively leaving as requisite, P 42 north of Lampedusa to position 35°41'N, 14°05'E and P 44 direct to position 35°31'N, 14°11'E to arrive 22/10. No submarine encountered is to be attacked unless definitely identified as hostile. (32)

19 Oct 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) attacked an enemy convoy and torpedoed and sank the Italian destroyer Giovanni da Verrazzano and the Italian transport Beppe (4459 GRT, built 1912) about 30 nautical miles south-west off Lampion Island in position 35°52'N, 12°05'E.
da Verrazzano had 20 killed or missing and 34 wounded, 255 from her as well as 38 survivors from Beppe were recovered.
[For the composition and movements of the convoy attacked see the event for 18 October 1942.]
1048 A/19 - Sighted aircraft to the northward on a southerly course zigzagging broadly.

1053 A/19 - Sighted the convoy bearing 360°. Enemy course was 180°. The ships were in two columns, two ships in each. The leading ships had tall funnels and were of 5000-7000 tons each. Aircraft were zigzagging ahead, the destroyer screen about a mile astern of the aircraft, then the leading ships followed by the rear ships at about a mile intervals.
1115 A/19 - The convoy altered course 156°. P 37 passed between the two port wing destroyers (Navigatore and Maestrale class) and attacked the leading ship in the port column.
1149 A/19 - Fired four torpedoes from 1000 yards. Explosions were head 1m 15s and 2m 5s after firing. P 37 had meanwhile gone deep.

1154 A/19 - Depth charging started. The counter attack was carried out by two destroyers stopping to listen from time to time. No Asdic transmissions were heard. Between 1200 and 1500 hours a total of 24 depth charges was dropped but only 2 were close.
1430 A/19 - Six or eight explosions were heard in rapid succession.
1440 A/19 - At periscope depth. One Maestrale-class destroyer was seen bearing 170° at a range of 3 miles. The enemy was apparently stopped. P 37 went deep again.
1740 A/19 - Returned to periscope depth. All was clear.
1900 A/19 - Surfaced.
2300 A/19 - Set course to return to Malta. (30)

19 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0210 A/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information regarding the convoy to HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN); Convoy in position 37°24'N, 11°53'E, course 180°, speed 8 knots at 0058 A/19.

In a signal timed 1130 A/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information regarding the convoy to the above mentioned submarines; My 0210 A/19. Convoy, composition unknown, course 180° in position 310° - Lampedusa - 35 miles. (32)
21 Oct 1942 At 1400 A/21, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
P 37 as well as HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN), were swept in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1030 A/21. (30)

1 Nov 1942 At 1030 A/1, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily, to the west of Cape Milazzo, to provide cover for Operation Torch, the Allied landings in North Africa.
She, and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) were swept out until around 1730 A/1 by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR). (11)
1 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 37 during her 9th war patrol see the map below.
2 Nov 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) made the westbound submerged passage of Q.B.B. 255 on this day. In a signal timed 0215 A/3, she reported her safe passage to the Capt. S.10. (11)

3 Nov 1942 At 1450 A/3, north-west of Cape San Vito, in position 38°20'N, 12°24'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), sighted a merchant vessel bearing 040° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 250°. Commenced attack.
On closing this ship was seen to be in ballast so torpedoes were not fired and the attack was broken off at 1545 A/3 and course was altered to 090°.
The vessel sighted was possibly the Italian transport Vertunno (3159 GRT, built 1909) which had sailed from Palermo at 0800 A/3 at 9 knots for Sousse where she arrived at 2100 A/4. (33)
4 Nov 1942 At 2000 A/4, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), arrived in her patrol position to the west of Cape Milazzo (38°20'N, 14°50'E) and commenced to patrol in the vicinity. (11)
6 Nov 1942 At 0200 A/6, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) picked up HE bearing 060° but this soon faded.
At 0248 A/6, HE was heard which was thought to be from a motor torpedo boat. HMS P 37 dived to evade. She remained dived until 1825 A/6. (34)

8 Nov 1942 At 1925 A/8, in approximate position 38°20'N, 14°47'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) picked up HE bearing 200°. This was thought to originate from a motor torpedo boat and HMS P 37 dived to evade surfacing again at 1950 A/8. (34)
11 Nov 1942 At 1906 A/11, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1729 A/11 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 38°10'N, 13°25'E (off Palermo).
At 2100 A/11, course was set to comply with these orders. (11)

12 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1745 A/12, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) of the following; At 1440 A/12, one merchant ship and two torpedo boats in position 39°20'N, 13°40'E, course 192°, speed 10 knots.

HMS P 37 received the signal at 1843 A/12 and course was altered to the westward in case these ships altered course to the westward. It is thought the enemy indeed did this just south of Ustica as they were not sighted and at 2230 A/12, HMS P 37 altered course to return to her patrol area off Palermo Bay.

This signal most probably referred to the German transport Menes (5610 GRT, built 1926) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Cigno and Calliope which had sailed from Naples at 0027 A/12-0030 A/12 and arrived at Palermo at 2158 A/12 and 0003 A/13 (Calliope). (35)

13 Nov 1942 Late in the evening HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) was detected by an Italian A/S vessel, thought to be a torpedo boat and briefly depth charged. Seven depth charges were dropped but they caused no damage to HMS P 37.
The motor torpedo boats MAS 549 and MAS 546 sailed from Palermo at 1005 A/13 to hunt a submarine and returned at 2350 A/13.

The A/S vessel that depth charged P 37, was the Italian torpedo boat Uragano, which had sailed from Palermo at 2200 A/13, to join the Italian transport Città di Tunisi (5419 GRT, built 1929), which, escorted by the destroyer Folgore, was en-route to Bizerte. They had sailed from Naples at 1131 A/13, also escorted by the destroyer Freccia but, the latter had steering gear defects, and at 1302 A/13 had to turn back. At 2315 A/13, Uragano sighted a surfaced submarine and made a reconnaissance signal as it could be Italian. It immediately dived in position 313° - Cape Gallo – 6 miles and the torpedo boat dropped seven 30-kg depth charges but did not linger in the area as she had to catch up with the convoy but due to the rain and poor visibility, she could only join at 1025 A/14, and they arrived at destination at 1200 A/14.
0500 A/13 - Dived into Palermo Bay.
0830 A/13 - Sighted a merchant vessel escorted by a motor torpedo boat entering harbour.

0900 A/13 - Motor torpedo boats appeared to be searching. Tappings on about 10kc's and small charges were heard and two motor torpedo boats were heard starting and stopping from time to time. Lt. Stanley decided to withdraw and establish patrol in position 340° - Cape Gallo - 4 miles.

1700 A/13 - A merchant vessel was seen to enter Palermo harbour from the northward. The range could not be closed to less than 6000 yards. The ship was unescorted until her arrival off Cape Gallo. Given the long range the ship was not considered worth firing at taking the attack restrictions into account.
2150 A/13 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1515 A/13 which contained the following orders; All merchant shipping in the vicinity of Palermo is to be attacked.

2215 A/13 - HE thought to be of a motor torpedo boat was heard approaching from the westward. Shortly afterwards one and then a second darkened vessel was sighted. One appeared about twice the size of the other so an attack was started on the largest vessel. The Asdic operator was convinced there was only fast diesel HE so the attack was broken off and P 37 turned away into the shadow of the land. On arrival off Palermo Bay, a smaller craft was seen to flash a code signal and part company. HE of the larger vessel was then heard which had apparently to the northward of the suspected motor torpedo boat. P 37 chased the target at full speed but the range could not be closed and patrol was resumed.

2310 A/13 - HE was reported approaching from the eastward. 230 revs. and 90 revs. were reported. Only one vessel was sighted and attacked. This unfortunately turned out to be a Spica-class torpedo boat doing 24 knots and not a merchant vessel at 12 knots as the HE had indicated. It was not possible to get off track before the enemy challenged by VF lights at a range of about 800 yards. P 37 crash dived and was counter attacked with seven depth charges. The enemy did not reduce speed though. (11)

14 Nov 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) attacked a 2000 ton merchant vessel with three torpedoes north of Palermo, Sicily. No hits were obtained. This was the much smaller Italian transport Ustica (403 GRT, 1911).

0720 A/14 - Started attack on a distant merchant vessel but this had to be broken off as a trawler and a motor torpedo boat appeared and started to hunt, stopping to listen and dropping small charges periodically. Lt. Stanley decided to withdraw to the track of this merchant vessel for the remainder of the day. She had passed Palermo eastbound well clear of the coast.
1400 A/14 - Sighted a 2000 ton merchant vessel. Started attack.
1455 A/14 - In approximate position 38°22'N, 13°18'E fired three torpedoes from 3000 yards. These all missed astern as the speed of the target was underestimated.
1800 A/14 - A destroyer and an 'E-boat' were heard approaching. The destroyer was transmitting on Asdic. 9 Depth charges were dropped. The destroyer remained hunting in the area for hours.
1900 A/14 - The destroyer was a bit further off now. Surfaced and was able to avoid the destroyer due to the fact that it was not perfectly darkened.
2151 A/14 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1501 A/14 ordering P 37 to patrol north of Marettimo in position 38°19'N, 12°00'E. Set course accordingly. (11)

14 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1501 A/14, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Establish patrol line by 0400 A/15, 3, 337° - Marettimo Light - 8, 360°, 7, 270°, 30. P 43 in southern billet and P 37 in northern. (36)
15 Nov 1942 At 0430 A/15, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) arrived in position 38°19'N, 12°00'E. (11)

16 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1619 A/16, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN). This signal was also repeated to HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); Collect report from patrol line, withdraw 20 miles north-west after dark and inform me on enemy traffic and route, torpedoes remaining and remarks of interest.

In a signal timed 1915 A/16, P 37 informed the Capt. S.10 of the following. This signal was also repeated to HMS Parthian and HMS P 43; Unable to collect report due to defective Asdic [S.S./T. communication was thus not possible]. Two motor torpedo boats course 200° only shipping sighted from my position. Numerous aircraft. Five torpedoes remaining. Torpedo boat only ship sighted leaving (Palermo?) westbound.

In a signal timed 2355 A/16, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to these three submarines; Move 36 miles 073° to establish identical patrol line from position 360° - Cape St. Vito - 3 miles by 0400 A/17. (37)

17 Nov 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) carried out a torpedo attack on a large escorted merchant vessel. No hits were obtained. This was the Italian passenger/cargo vessel Città di Napoli (5418 GRT, built 1930) escorted by the torpedo boat Clio.

Città di Napoli and her escort, the torpedo boat Clio had sailed from Bizerte at 1930 A/16. At 0705 A/17, they sighted four torpedo tracks from astern on the port side, and Città di Napoli turned to starboard to avoid them. Clio dropped a number of depth charges but decided not to linger and caught up with the transport. They arrived at Palermo at 1021 A/17.
0600 A/17 - Arrived in position 38°27'N, 12°43'E.
0640 A/17 - Sighted a 10000 ton transport ship escorted by a torpedo boat. Started attack.
0701 A/17 - Fired four torpedoes from 1500 yards. No hits were obtained as the enemy altered course while zig-zagging. No counter attack followed. (11)

17 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1045 A/17, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN) and HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN). HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) has withdrawn. Move with all despatch to establish identical patrol line 5 miles 180° from present positions. (37)

17 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1352 A/17, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN); After dark tonight P 42 move through position 38°06'N, 11°42'E to position 360° - Cape San Vito - 4 miles. At 0001 A/18 Parthian and P 37 move to establish patrol line in position 360° 7 and 14 miles respectively from P 42. (37)

18 Nov 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) attacked but missed a merchant vessel that was part of a convoy to the north of Cape San Vito. Only one torpedo could be fired as this was P 37's last one on board.

The target was the Italian transport Viminale (8657 GRT, built 1925) which was in company with the Italian transport Puccini (2422 GRT, built 1928). They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Legionario and Bombardiere and the italian torpedo boat Groppo. They had sailed from Palermo at 0445 A/18-0555 A/18 and arrived at Bizerte at 0900 A/19-0944 A/19.

1235 A/18 - While patrolling at 80 feet due to the rough sea HE was reported bearing 060°. Came to periscope depth. In approximate position 38°23'N, 12°42'E sighted a 7000 ton merchant vessel bearing 075° at a range of only 200 yards. Went to 60 feet to avoid collision. Returned to periscope depth after the HE had passed.

1246 A/18 - Fired the last remaining torpedo from 1500 yards. It missed. A convoy of three merchant vessels escorted by two destroyers was sighted. Afterwards two attempts to sent a contact report were spoiled by approaching aircraft and it was almost three hours later before the signal could be passed.
1522 A/18 - Passed P 37's signals timed 1233 A/18 and 1246 A/18 which contained the following; One convoy in position 38°23'N, 12°41'E, course 270°, speed 13 knots.
In a reply timed 1445 A/18 the Capt. S.10 sent the following; Your 1233 A/18. Make amplifying report, type ship, number funnels of important unit.
1800 A/18 - Out of torpedoes, P 37 set course to return to Malta.

1830 A/18 - Passed P 37's signal timed 1645 A/18 which contained the following; Your 1445 A/18. Details doubtful. At least three fairly large cargo vessels 130 revs per minute. Torpedoes expended. Returning through position 330° - Marittimo Light on the 19th.

In a reply timed 2154 A/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to P 37; Your 1645 A/18. Dive at position 'B' at 0430 A/18. You are to arrive at position 'F' at dawn/20 and make rendezvous with the minesweeper at 1335 A/20. You are not to cross 36°00'N before 0700 A/20. (11)
19 Nov 1942 At 0005 A/19, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 180° at a range of about 400 to 500 yards. Dived. This was thought to be HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN).
At 0008 A/18, P 42 sighted a submarine fine on the port bow. The submarine was steering a south-westerly course. The other submarine soon dived. It was considered to have been HMS P 37. (11)
19 Nov 1942 At 0430 A/19, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) dived to commence the eastbound passage of the Q.B.B. 255 mined area. She surfaced at 1820 A/19.
At 2243 A/19, P 37 dived for suspected motor torpedo boats surfacing again at 2322 A/19. (33)
20 Nov 1942 At 1650 A/20, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 1300 A/20. (11)

27 Nov 1942 Around 0955 A/27, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of the Gulf of Tunis to intercept enemy traffic to Tunis and Bizerta.
She was escorted out until 1200 A/27 by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR). (11)
