27 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 37 during her 10th war patrol see the map below.
28 Nov 1942 At 0548 A/28, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) dived for the westbound passage of Q.B.B. 255 [mined area].
At 1400 A/28, in position 37°36'N, 12°35'E and while at 80 feet, HMS P 37 hit an obstruction.
At 2312 A/28, HMS P 37 surfaced in position 37°45'N, 11°55'E. (11)
29 Nov 1942 At 1623 A/29, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1254 A/29 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 37°30'N. 10°30'E.
At 1954 A/29, HMS P 37 dived on sighting a submarine which was assumed to be friendly. [Most likely this was indeed the case.]
At 2010 A/29, HMS P 37 surfaced.
At 2200 A/29, HMS P 37 arrived in the ordered patrol position (37°30'N. 10°30'E). (11)

30 Nov 1942 At 1822 A/30, north of the Gulf of Tunis, in position 37°31'N, 10°33'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sighted an Italian Spica-class torpedo boat proceeding on course 070°. The enemy was sweeping on Asdic.
[We have been unable to find any torpedo boat or destroyer in this area at this time.] (11)

1 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1111 A/1, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN) and HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); Move patrol positions, P 219 7 miles and P 37 4 miles both 180° from the previous ordered positions. (38)
1 Dec 1942 At 0600 A/1, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sighted an illuminated hospital ship passing through position 37°16'N, 10°32'E. This hospital ship was most likely bound for Bizerta.
Perhaps this was the Italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) which sailed from Naples at 1930 A/29 and arrived in Tripoli at 0820 A/2.
At 1000 A/1, HMS P 37 sighted smoke to the southward. She was not able to close sufficiently to identify the source of this smoke.
At 1622 A/1, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1111 A/1 was received in which P 37 was ordered to ships patrol 4 miles 180°. Set course to comply with this order.
At 1645 A/1, two Spica-class torpedo boats were seen passing through position 37°30'N, 10°30'E. Enemy course was 070°, speed 12 knots. Both were sweeping on Asdic.

This were most probably the Italian torpedo boats Partenope and Perseo which had sailed from Trapani at 0152 A/1-0155 A/1 for an A/S sweep off Cape Bon. At 1410 A/1, Partenope located a submarine with her sonar in position 37°23'N, 10°09'E. She dropped nine depth charges at 1421 A/1 and another six at 1432 A/1 without observing any result. At 1600 A, the two torpedo boats proceeded for an anti-mine sweep. At 0905 A/2, in position 37°45'N, 10°57'E, they recovered ten survivors from the transport Aventino (3794 GRT, built 1907), sunk by Force Q [made up of the light cruisers HMS Aurora (Capt. Sir W.G. Agnew, CB, RN), HMS Sirius (Capt. P.B.W. Brooking, RN), HMS Argonaut (Capt. E.W.L. Longley-Cook, RN) and the destroyers HMAS Quiberon (Cdr. H.W.S. Browning, OBE, RN) and HMS Quentin (Lt.Cdr. A.H.P. Noble, RN)]. They returned to Trapani at 1829 B/2-1940 B/2.
At 2150 A/1, a motor torpedo boat passed at close range. This vessel was eastbound.

At 2345 A/1, HE was heard to the northward where there was a very dark background. It was believed this HE was from a submarine. While turning away the Officer of the Watch and both lookouts considered that two torpedo tracks passed close ahead. The sea was very phosphorescent however and there were a lot of porpoises in the vicinity which may have caused the tracks. (11)

2 Dec 1942 Between 0200 A/2 and 0400 A/2, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sighted starshell fired by ' Force Q ', which was made up of the light cruisers HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, CB, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral C.H.J. Harcourt, CBE, RN), HMS Argonaut (Capt. E.W.L. Longley-Cook, RN), HMS Sirius (Capt. P.W.B. Brooking, RN) and the destroyers HMS Quentin (Lt.Cdr. A.H.P. Noble, DSC, RN) and HMAS Quiberon (Cdr. H.W.S. Browning, OBE, RN) when they were attacking and decimating an enemy convoy.
At 0745 A/2, two Spica-class torpedo boats passed well to the southward on a north-easterly course.
This were most likely the Italian torpedo boats Partenope and Perseo. [See the event of 1 December 1942 for their movements.]

At 1315 A/2, HMS P 37 started an attack on a 300-tons schooner. As this schooner was eastbound and the range could not be closed sufficiently to make certain of hitting it, Lt. Stanley decided not to fire torpedoes. A continuous stream of aircraft overhead made a gun action undesirable.
At 1700 A/2, a motor torpedo boat resembling the Stefano Turr passed close to the westward bound for Bizerta. (11)

3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2359 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); P 217 and P 219 proceed to the Naples area with despatch by proceeding on surface by day at the Commanding Officers discretion to patrol within 8 miles of the following positions; P 217, 40°47'N, 13°48'E and P 219, 40°35'N, 14°08'E. HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) is expected to join you shortly.

P 37, P 43 and P 48 proceed with despatch but submerged by day to form a patrol line (operation B.S.). 3 - 350° -Cape San Vito, with the first submarine 4 miles from Cape San Vito and the other submarine spaced 5 miles apart.
P 45 return to establish patrol approximately 40° from Plane Island lighthouse in position selected by you for interception of Bizerta traffic. (38)

3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0438 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); On completion of attacks [on an expected convoy] you are to withdraw either the the north-east to the Tyrrhenian Sea or via the route of HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN) as in my 0005 A/1 to the south-east of Pantelleria.

[HMS Trooper's route had been through the following positions according the signal timed 0005 A/1 from the Capt. S.10; 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E), 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E), 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E).]

In a signal timed 0451 A/3, these submarines were sent the following orders; Assume the following dispositions forthwith; P 219, 37°28'N, 11°00'E. P 37, 37°24'N, 10°51'E. P 45, 37°13'N, 10°25'E. P 48 and P 43, remain in your present ordered positions. Position, course and speed of the enemy convoy at 0205 A/3, 37°50'N, 11°38'E, 235°, 8 knots. Convoy of four merchant ships and five destroyers.

In a signal timed 0845 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37, HMS P 43 and HMS P 48; Move with utmost despatch from ordered position as follows; P 37, 10 miles 305°. P 48, 5 miles 305°. P 43, 5 miles 340°. (38)
3 Dec 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) attacked an enemy convoy with four torpedoes fired from long range. No hits were obtained.

This convoy consisted of the Italian transports Arlesiana (5702 GRT, built 1915), Achille Lauro (5209 GRT, built 1921), Campania (5247 GRT, built 1918) and the German transport Menes (5609 GRT, built 1926). The German transport Lisboa (1799 GRT, built 1911) was also to have been part of this convoy but she apparently did not sail, perhaps due to defects (?). The convoy sailed from Trapani at 1700 A/2-1814 A/2, escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sirio (escort leader), Groppo, Uragano and Orione. At 0735 A/3, they were reinforced by two German S-boats (out of the following, S 58, S 60, S 56 and S 54, which had sailed from Bizerte at 2115 A/2). At 0230 A/3, the Italian torpedo boat Animoso sailed from Trapani to reinforce the escort. At 0830 A/3, she sighted the convoy and took her place in the screen.
The attack was noticed by a German aircraft, which fired machine-gun bursts at sea at 1118 A (?). Groppo was ordered to hunt the submarine.

At 1138 A/3, Achille Lauro and Arlesiana, escorted by Orione and Animoso, were detached to Tunis. At 1000 A/3, the torpedo boat Antonio Mosto sailed from Tunis to join the escort of the Tunis section. At 1225 A/3, she was hit by a torpedo from HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) which failed to explode. Shortly after, she joined the convoy.

Campania and Menes, escorted by Sirio and Uragano, proceeded to Bizerte. At about the same time, the torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori and two German S-boats were sighted and reinforced the Bizerte section. Giuseppe Sirtori had sailed from Bizerte at 0855 A/3 for minesweeping duty but was diverted to this new task. At 1235 A/3, Groppo rejoined them and was sent to escort the Tunis section, while Animoso was ordered to switch back to the Bizerte section. At 1330 A/3, Menes hit a mine and blew up. The four S-boats remained in the area to pick up the survivors. Campania escorted by Sirio, Uragano and Animoso arrived in Bizerte at 1610 A/3-1703 A/3.
Achille Lauro and Arlesiana, escorted by Groppo, Orione, Antonio Mosto and Giuseppe Sirtori, arrived in Tunis at 1715 A/3-1737 A/3.

0730 A/3 - Sighted a topsail schooner to the northward. It appeared from enemy reports that HMS P 37 would only just have time to get into position before the expected enemy convoy would arrive and an attack on this schooner would have made this impossible, the schooner was not attacked. This proved unfortunate as the expected convoy would follow the route of the schooner fairly closely.
1001 A/3 - Sighted smoke and escorting aircraft of a convoy to the northward. Set course to intercept at full speed. A schooner, two destroyers and four merchant ships were sighted.

1037 A/3 - In approximate position 37°26'N, 10°43'E fired four torpedoes at two of the merchant vessels that were overlapping from 9000 yards. No hits were obtained. it had not been possible to close the range further.
1820 A/3 - Set course for the Tyrrhenian Sea.

Between 2200 A/3 and midnight - Sighted three lighted ships, apparently patrolling, 20 miles south-west of Marittimo. Also an E-boat was sighted. One ship was was almost certainly seen to be a hospital ship. They were not closed to within three miles as they were suspected of being decoys. (11)
4 Dec 1942 At 0138 A/4, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2359 A/3.
At 0556 A/4, HMS P 37 dived in position 38°10'N, 12°20'E.
At 0800 A/4, while to the north-west of Trapani, in position 38°13'N, 12°17'E, HMS P 37 sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat which passed close by on an easterly course at high speed.
This was most probably the Italian torpedo boat Perseo which sailed from Trapani at 1829 A/3 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1104 A/4.
At 1400 A/4, HMS P 37 arrived in the ordered position, near Cape San Vito (38°15'N, 12°43'E). (11)

5 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1058 A/5, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); Withdraw 50 miles from the coast, orders will follow. (38)
5 Dec 1942 At 1305 A/5, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1058 A/5.
At 1630 A/5, course was set to the northward to comply with the orders received. (11)
6 Dec 1942 At 0100 A/6, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) commenced patrolling in the vicinity of position 38°55'N, 12°15'E. (11)

8 Dec 1942 At 1852 A/8, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1333 A/8 which contained the following orders; Return from patrol after dark tonight by route in my signal timed 0005/1. Keep to your starboard side of the route. Pass positions 'J' to 'K' during daylight/10. Instructions for arrival at Malta will be signalled.
At 1925 A/8, HMS P 37 set course to return to Malta. (39)
10 Dec 1942 At 0100 A/10, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) started the passage of Q.B.B. 255 [mined area].
At 0617 A/10, HMS P 37 dived.
At 2030 A/10, HMS P 37 surfaced in position 36°35'N, 11°50'E. (40)

10 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0940 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); Make rendezvous with motor launch at 0802 A/11 in position 36°02'N, 14°01'E whence you will be escorted into harbour. You are not to be south of 36°00'N before rendezvous is made. Gozo Light north-west sector will be shown from 0400 A/11 until 0600 A/11. You may meet HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Parthian (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) westbound on your route tonight. (38)
12 Dec 1942 At 1225 A/12, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Parthian (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined around 1040 A/12. (11)

26 Dec 1942 At 1300 A/26, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to conduct a special operation off the Tunisian coast.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 37 during this patrol see the map below.
28 Dec 1942 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) carried out her special operation.

In the evening French agents Walter Borg and Charles Gomand were landed on the Tunisian coast south of Kelibia near the mouth of the Udiane river. Their folbots capsized in the surf but they reached the shore without difficulty. Two British soldiers (Sergeant Casey and Private Greghan) who were helping them and were to bring the folbots back to the submarine had to be left behind. After waiting for them in vain, the submarine left the scene. The two soldiers were assisted by the Frenchmen and later managed to reach Allied lines.
[Our thanks go to Guy Debbasch who helped identify the two French agents.]
0300 A/28 - Sighted the Tunisian coast. During the forenoon the coastline was studied through the periscope and a spot was selected for the landing of the agents.
1040 A/28 - Bottomed four miles off the coast in position 36°43'N, 11°03'E.

1525 A/28 - Heard HE of a destroyer proceeding southwards down the coast. This vessel was transmitting on Asdic. It was considered that if she was escorting any ship large enough to merit attacking (and consequently delaying the landing operation at least 48 hours), it would be audible. As nothing was heard it was decided to remain on the bottom as this would render detection less likely.
1540 A/28 - On reaching a position due west of P 37, the destroyer turned round and proceeded north again.

1600 A/28 - Came to periscope depth to have a last look at the coast during daylight. Then bottomed again. Two small ships of 1000-1500 tons were sighted close inshore proceeding southwards escorted by four aircraft.
1823 A/28 - Surfaced and lay stopped and prepared the for hatch for opening. It was intended to place the Folbots on the casing and load them there and then proceed inshore to the launching position.
1835 A/28 - Heard HE of a destroyer or motor torpedo boat to the eastward. Dived to evade.
2010 A/28 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1741 A/28. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
2018 A/28 - Surfaced 1 mile from the coast in 6 fathoms.
2045 A/28 - Both Folbots were away and were seen to be making for the selected position about 1.5 miles south of the mouth of the Wadi Uniane (36°43'N, 10°58'E).
2058 A/28 - Proceeded half a mile to seaward and bottomed in 10 fathoms.
2120 A/28 - Surfaced to wait for the return of the two Folbots.
2230 A/28 - The moon rose.
2245 A/28 - The moon was now silhouetting the submarine fairly clearly. Lt. Stanley decided to wait for 15 minutes in case the boats were still searching and the light of the moon would assist them.
2300 A/28 - Abandoned the hope of recovering the Folbots. Set course to pass through Q.B.B. 255 [mined area]. (11)
29 Dec 1942 At 0300 A/29, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 2259 A/18 in which she reported the result of the special operations.

At 2047 A/29, HMS P 37 received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1002 A/29 [or 1012 A/29, sources vary] which contained the following orders; Cancel patrol orders proceed from position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) direct to patrol in the vicinity of position 38°50'N, 09°15'E (off Cagliari Bay, Sardinia). Attack shipping during passage but do not compromise final position. Further orders will follow. (39)

30 Dec 1942 When en-route to her patrol area, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) was detected and depth charged by an Italian torpedo-boat. Depth charges fell very close causing some minor damage to P 37.
This was the Italian torpedo boat Perseo which had sailed from Trapani with her sister ship Partenope at 1355 A/29-1410 A/29 for an A/S sweep, ahead of the passage of convoys.

At 1934 A/29, Partenope obtained a good echo at a range of 1600 metres and dropped five patterns of nine depth charges each at 1940 A/29, 1948 A/29, 1957 A/29, 2116 A/29 and 2130 A/29 and another six depth charges at 2142 A/29 (three depth charges could not be dropped as they were not ready in time due to the handling in the darkness.
At 0829 A/30, in position 271° - Marettimo – 29 miles, Perseo obtained a good sonar contact and attacked with depth charges. She rejoined Partenope at 1048 A.
At 0505 A/31, Perseo was detached to replace the destroyer Freccia which had developed defects while escorting a convoy (see 31 December 1942).
At 0550 A/31, in position 37°53'N, 11°27'E, Partenope obtained a good echo with her three remaining depth charges. Partenope returned to Trapani at 1300 A/31.

0817 A/30 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat in position 37°58'N, 11°12'E at a range of 5000 yards. She was first mistaken for a U-boat. Course was altered to avoid her but unfortunately she detected P 37.
0839 A/30 - The first pattern of depth charges was dropped which was unpleasantly close causing some minor damage. P 37 was at 150 at that time.
0853 A/30 - A pattern of seven depth charges was dropped a bit further off. The torpedo-boat was still in contact.
0915 A/30 - A pattern of six depth charges was dropped, again not very close.
1015 A/30 - Contact with the enemy was lost but Asdic transmissions were heard until 1100 A/30.
1245 A/30 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.

1305 A/30 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1055 A/30 which contained the following orders for her; Endeavour to be in position 37°55'N, 11°21'E from 0001 A/31 to 0601 A/31. You must expect torpedo boat patrols in this area and also targets about midnight. Afterwards continue to Cagliari patrol position. (11)

30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2209 A/30, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); Aircraft are patrolling your area tonight after moonrise in endeavour to bomb enemy torpedo boats but enemy aircraft must be expected hunting you. (38)
31 Dec 1942 At 0001 A/31, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) arrived on the expected track of the enemy convoy.

The expected convoy was made up of the German transports Rhea (1386 GRT, built 1922), Ruhr (5955 GRT, built 1926) and Stella (former French Saint Sauveur, former Danish Stella, 1394 GRT, built 1933), escorted by the Italian destroyer Freccia and the Italian torpedo boats Animoso, Ardente and Ardito. This convoy had sailed from Palermo at 1535 A/30-1800 A/30. Freccia developed engine defects and had to be detached to Trapani where she arrived at 0735 A/31. She was replaced by the Italian torpedo boat Perseo which joined at 0434 A/31. She had been carrying an A/S sweep off Marittimo with the torpedo boat Partenope (see entry for 30 December 1942). At 1150 A/31, the convoy split in two: 1) Stella and Rhea, escorted by Perseo and Animoso, arrived in Tunis at 1700 A/31-2030 A/31. 2) Ruhr, escorted by Ardente and Ardito arrived in Bizerte at 1710 A/31-1830 A/31. [The information on this convoy was ULTRA-related (see file DEFE3/791/frames 456, 493, 533, etc.].

Between 0200 A/31 and 0330 A/31, aircraft flares were sighted to the eastward which indicated that the convoy was passing well to the eastward, close to Marittimo Island. An attempt to close at full speed for 1.5 hours was unsuccessful. While doing so P 37 was put down twice by Very white flares being dropped fairly close which were assumed to be enemy but the aircraft which dropped them did not remain in the vicinity for long.
At 06001 A/31, course was set for the patrol position of Cagliari (38°50'N, 09°15'E). (11)
1 Jan 1943 At 1200 A/1, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) arrived in her ordered patrol position (38°50'N, 09°15'E) and commenced to patrol in the vicinity.

At 2221 A/1, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2032 A/1 was received which contained the following orders; Establish patrol off Cagliari north of 38°53'N and west of 10°00'W. You have freedom of action regarding targets. Traffic with Italy passes usually inside Cavoli Island.
At 2300 A/1, HMS P 37 set course for a position to the east of the Cavoli Island lighthouse. (41)
4 Jan 1943 At 1424 A/4, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) commenced an attack on a vessel.
At 1455 A/4, the attack was broken off when the target was identified as an Italian RD-class minesweeper. (42)
5 Jan 1943 At 0925 A/5, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) commenced an attack on a vessel.
At 0942 A/5, the attack was broken off when the target was identified as an Italian RD-class minesweeper.

At 2000 A/5, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1705 A/5 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol P.M./6 and proceed direct to position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) thence through positions 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E), 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E) to arrive at the end of the Malta searched channel at 1500 A/9 by route given my signal timed 1145 A/5. You are to adjust speed to make the passage between positions 'J' and 'K' submerged during daylight/8.

The route given in the signal timed 1145 A/5 was as follows; Owing to operations of friendly ASV aircraft south of latitude 36°00'N, the route from position 'K' to northwards will until further orders is to be through position 36°08'N, 14°04'E to arrive end of the swept channel at 1300 A. (43)

6 Jan 1943 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) attacked a small armed vessel with gunfire to the East of Cape Carbonara. The action was broken off, before hits were obtained, when the enemy returned fire.

This was the Italian auxiliary patrol boat AS 56 / Nuova Vincenzina on her way to meet the submarine Nichelio arriving from Naples. The enemy vessel was only armed with a single 8mm machine gun but despite being heavily outgunned and hit by one round from the submarine, it put up a fierce resistance forcing the submarine to withdraw.
1300 A/6 - In approximate position 39°08'N, 09°43'E sighted a small vessel approaching from seaward. She looked rather like a British Motor Minesweeper, armed only with a machine gun.

1415 A/6 - Surfaced and opened fire from 800 yards with the 12pdr gun. The first round was over and the next two just short. The enemy then opened fire with a heavy machine gun so the action was broken off as the target was judged not to be worth casualties. P 37 dived and the enemy made off inshore dropping 10 depth charges as he went.
1450 A/6 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
1500 A/6 - Set course for Malta. (11)

7 Jan 1943 At 1300 A/7, in position 37°46'N, 11°15'E, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sighted two destroyers or torpedo boats and a low flying aircraft. The destroyers were steering to the south-west and transmitting on Asdic. The destroyers turned round after an hour and dropped 40 depth charges between 1500 A/7 and 1700 A/7 on was was probably a non-sub echo.

The vessels sighted were the Italian torpedo boats Partenope and Perseo which had sailed from Trapani at 0515 A/7-0540 A/7 for an A/S sweep. At 1311 A/7, in position 360° - Cape Bon – 39 miles, Partenope obtained an echo on her sonar and at dropped six patterns of nine depth charges each at 1322 A/7, 1332 A/7, 1344 A/7, 1429 A/7, 1439 A/7 and 1449 A/7, observing an oil slick (also observed by a German aircraft on an A/S search). At 1450 A, Partenope assumed the submarine to be damaged and ordered Perseo to take over the hunt, but the latter lost contact at 1556 A/7. At 0530 A/8, Marina Trapani ordered Partenope to proceed to Tunis where she dropped anchor at 1100 A/8 to await orders to escort a convoy of three ships. Perseo was ordered to proceed to Palermo and arrived there at 1410 A/8.
At 2000 A/7, HMS P 37 started the passage of Q.B.B. 255 [mined area].
At 2215 A/7, HMS P 37 sighted HMS Abdiel (Capt. D. Orr-Ewing, RN) proceeding westwards. At 0315 A/8, she was seen again but now eastbound. HMS Abdiel was on a minelaying mission. (11)

8 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1231 A/8, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); Two fleet destroyers will be operating between Malta and Kuriat tonight. You are not to cross 36°00'N before dawn. They have orders not to attack submarines north of this latitude.
This referred to an anti-shipping sweep during then night of 8/9 January by the destroyers HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC, RN) and HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN). (44)
9 Jan 1943 At 1300 A/9, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)
11 Jan 1943 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (42)
16 Jan 1943 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) is undocked. (42)
20 Jan 1943 At 1310 A/20, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the south of the Straits of Messina.
She was routed through positions 35°20'N, 14°14'E and 35°20'N, 15°40'E thence direct to patrol area between latitudes 37°15'N, and 38°00'N and west of longitude 15°46'E.

22 Jan 1943 At 0400 A/22, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) dived in position 37°52'N, 15°52'E and established patrol 1 to 1.5 miles from the Calabrian coast between longitudes 15°40'E and 15°45'E. At 1800 A/22, HMS P 37 proceeded clear of the coast to charge due to the full moon condition. (11)

23 Jan 1943 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian passenger / cargo ship Viminale (8657 GRT, built 1925) and sank the Italian tug Luni (337 GRT, built 1914) south-east of Cape dell'Armi, Italy in position 37°52'N, 15°45'E.

Viminale had been damaged at Palermo by a British Chariot launched from HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN) during operation Principal. She was being towed from Messina (departed at 0400 A/23) to Taranto by the tugs Luni, Costante (294 GRT, built 1916) and Salvatore I (716 GRT, built 1902) and was escorted by the torpedo boat Perseo. Luni blew up and had thirty killed. Viminale was hit by two torpedoes, she was beached near Porto Salvo, she had a number of casualties. Perseo hunted the submarine and was assisted the auxiliary submarine chaser Quarnaro and four VAS boats.

At 0825 A/23, torpedo tracks were observed and a minute later, an escorting aircraft dropped a bomb. Viminale was struck by two torpedoes, and assisted by the tugs, was beached near Melito. Luni was sunk (30 killed) and her survivors were picked up by the VAS boats. By 1048 A/23, Perseo had expended all her depth charges. At 1147 A/23, the submarine chaser Quarnaro arrived and joined the search with Perseo and the four VAS. At 1525 A/23, the submarine chaser A.S. 67 supplied 23 depth charges to Perseo and the hunt continued.

At 1400 A/24, the Lubiana-convoy was sighted, and two hours later, the submarine chase was over, and Perseo ordered Quarnaro to return home and then proceeded with the four VAS-boats to escort this convoy. The Italian tanker Thorsheimer (9955 GRT, built 1935) escorted by the destroyer Lubiana and the torpedo boat Francesco Stocco had sailed from Taranto at 2140 A/23. However, at 1445 A/24, the torpedo boat signalled that she could not complete her mission due to a defective condenser. The convoy arrived at Messina at 1915 A/24-1920 A/24.
0120 A/23 - Dived and closed the coast to patrol on the same route as the previous day.
0630 A/23 - Sighted two motor torpedo boats coming from the eastward. They stopped near P 37.
0700 A/23 - Sighted a large ship coming from the Straits.
0715 A/23 - Identified the target as a large merchant ship in tow of two tugs escorted by a Spica-class torpedo-boat, several smaller vessels and a floatplane. Started attack.

0820 A/23 - Fired three torpedoes from 2000 yards at the merchant vessel. Torpedo explosions were heard 1m 35s and 1m 50s after firing. Following the attack, the torpedo-boat obtained contact and after 5 minutes dropped an accurate pattern of depth charges causing serious damage to P 37 which was then taken close to the shore where non-sub echos would confuse the enemy and after two more fairly close depth charge patterns it was possible to escape to the south-westward. Fifty-six depth charges in all were dropped.
1200 A/23 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight but the enemy could still be heard transmitting.

1830 A/23 - Surfaced and cleared the area to the south. It was found out that several cells of the battery were cracked. This section of the battery was disconnected and course was set towards Malta to effect repairs. (11)
25 Jan 1943 At 1000 A/25, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)

4 Feb 1943 At 1500 A/4, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the southern Adriatic between latitudes 40°10'N and 42°10'N.

8 Feb 1943 At 0430 A/8, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) dived in position 40°15'N, 19°03'E and established patrol on the direct route from Brindisi to the Corfu Channel which HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) had found profitable in January.
At 2130 A/8, having seen nothing all day, HMS P 37 set course for Monopoli. (11)

9 Feb 1943 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian auxiliary magnetic minesweeper Eritrea (2517 GRT, built 1918) 5 nautical miles bearing 103° of Monopoli, Italy in position 40°56'N, 17°36'E. The Italian torpedo boat Tifone was sent to hunt the submarine but mistakenly depth charged the wreck of the torpedo boat Confienza.

Eritrea had sailed from Ancona at 0600 A/8 for Brindisi. Following the sinking the torpedo boat Tifone sailed from Brindisi at 1605 A/9. At 1655 A/10, an echo was detected at a range of 1500 metres. The submarine appeared to try to escape at 5 knots. At 1715 A/9, in position 40°44'N, 17°55'E, she dropped three patterns of three depth charges each. Ten minutes later, she observed an oil slick 50 metres wide. and returned at 1356 A/10. At 1758 A/10, she dropped another three patterns of three depth charges each. The submarine appeared to be lying on the bottom and, at 1817 A/10, two patterns of three depth charges each were thrown. At 1823 A/10, this was followed by one last pattern of three depth charges. She returned to Brindisi at 1356 A/11.
0546 A/9 - Dived in position 40°55'N, 17°28'E and established patrol 3 miles from the Italian coast.

0855 A/9 - Sighted a 2500 ton merchant ship approaching from the northward. The ship was not escorted and was not zigging so she was an easy target, as when she was sighted, P 37 was about 12 nautical miles ahead of her.

0927 A/9 - Fired three torpedoes from 900 yards. The second torpedo hit abreast the after end of the superstructure after a running time of 47 seconds. The crew abandoned ship most efficiently all boats being clear of the ship within 6 minutes after the torpedo hit. She did not appear to be sinking very fast.
0950 A/9 - Fired another torpedo from 600 yards. It missed as the torpedo failed to run straight probably due to a gyro failure.
1010 A/9 - The target was seen to sink. Set course for the eastern shore of the Adriatic. (11)

10 Feb 1943 HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian passenger ship Carlo Margottini (854 GRT, built 1893, former Yugoslav Bled) in the Adriatic Sea south of Antivari (now Bar), Montenegro near position 41°57'N, 19°07'E. This ship had two wounded and ran ashore and was later declared a total loss.

She carried 120 passengers (including 113 Military) and a crew of 39 (all Yugoslav nationals except five Italian naval gunners, the executive officer and the radio operator). Initially, she had sailed from Durazzo with stops in San Giovanni di Medua (Shëngjin, Albania) and Dulcigno from where she had just sailed at 1345 A/10 for Antivari. She was beached in the bay of Valle di Noce (aka Valdinos). Her gun fired about 50 rounds at the periscope of the attacking submarine. Coastal batteries fired for two hours at the submarine, and a seaplane from Teodo was sent to hunt the submarine but did not see anything.

The prompt action of her crew in beaching her certainly saved many lives. It was feared that the enemy might try to board her and snatch secret documents, but the explosion of the last torpedo had blocked access to the radio room (testimony of the radio operator Gaetano CAMERA). At about 1525 A/10, the German motor minesweepers R 169 and R 197 arrived at Valle di Noce and went alongside the stricken ship but did not take part in the action.
0510 A/10 - Dived in position 41°55'N, 19°04'E and established patrol 3 miles off the Yugoslav coast between latitudes 41°56'N and 42°02'E.
1215 A/10- Sighted a ship approaching from the southward. An earlier ship coming from the southward was not attacked in the hope that this ship was more valuable.
1310 A/10 - Started attack. The target was seen to be of about 1500 tons.

1403 A/10 - Fired two torpedoes from 1500 yards. Both missed astern as the targets speed was underestimated. Both torpedoes exploded on the rocks behind. The target then altered course 180°. The target then disappeared into a bay.
1456 A/10 - P 37 was taken into the bay and fired another torpedo from 1800 yards. There was no result as the torpedo most likely failed to run straight.
1510 A/10 - Fired the last remaining torpedo from 1500 yards. It hit abreast the mainmast. P 37 then cleared the bay and out of torpedoes set course towards Malta. (11)

12 Feb 1943 At 1900 A/12, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 1805 A/12 to the Capt. S.10, which contained the following; Torpedoes expended. Expect me at Lazaretto at 1200 A/14. [A weather report was also included in the signal.] (45)
14 Feb 1943 At 1535 A/14, HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)

27 Feb 1943 At 1300 A/27, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation. Several beach reconnaissances were to be carried out in preparation for Operation Husky.
28 Feb 1943 During the night of 28 February / 1 March, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) carried out a special operation (reconnaissance of Beach 28 [near Porto Palo].
0530 A/28 - Dived in position 37°07'N, 13°17'E and proceeded towards Beach 28 while running the M.D.U. (Mine Detection Unit) intermittently.
1736 A/28 - HMS Unbending was now in position 37°32'N, 12°53'E. Carried out a periscope reconnaissance of Beach 28.
2005 A/28 - Surfaced 2 miles from the coast and launched a folbot manned by A/Lt.Cdr. N.J.M. Teacher, DSO, RN and Lt. N.W. Cooper, RNVR.
2015 A/28 - Bottomed.
2335 A/28 - Surfaced and started the homing procedure with infra-red lamp.

0035 A/29 - Lt. Cooper returned alone. He had allowed A.Lt.Cdr. Teacher 59 minutes after the agreed time of rendezvous and then decided that to remain longer was only needlessly compromising his own and the submarines safety. The Folbot had waited 150 yards from the beach in ideal weather conditions. A/Lt.Cdr. Teacher had accustomed himself to remaining up to three hours in the water but this night's operation (being the first), was planned only to allow him 2 hours. Lt. Cooper heard no sounds of fighting on the beach.

It seems most likely the A/Lt.Cdr. Teacher's suit flooded up and he caught cramp and drifted down the coast with the easterly set. It is known that this suit was too small for him and he might easily have torn it slightly, thereby causing flooding, as Capt. Burbidge did on a subsequent occasion. It is thus believed A/Lt.Cdr. Teacher eventually drowned.
0045 A/29 - Dived for an ASV contsact and proceeded to seaward.
0310 A/29 - Surfaced and commenced to charge the batteries.
1 Mar 1943 During the night of 1/2 March, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) carried out a special operation (reconnaissance of Beach 28 [near Porto Palo].
0535 A/1 - Dived and spent most of the day bottomed two miles from the coast. A small fleet of fishing craft were operating in the vicinity, some being about the size of east coast drifters.
1935 A/1 - Launched the Folbot.
Between 0001 A/2 and 0030 A/2, a diesel engined craft passed to the eastward. This vessel was westbound and may have been a submarine.
It is unlikely that this had been a submarine. It may well have been the German motor torpedo boat S 60 which was en-route from Porto Empedocle to Ferryville, Tunisia during the night of 1/2 March.
0038 A/1 - The Folbot returned and HMS Unbending retired to seaward to charge. (46)
2 Mar 1943 During the night of 2/3 March, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) carried out a special operation (reconnaissance of Beach 28 [near Porto Palo].
1924 A/2 - Launched the Folbot.
0100 A/2 - The Folbot returned and HMS Unbending retired to seaward to charge the battery. (11)
3 Mar 1943 During the night of 3/4 March, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) carried out a special operation (reconnaissance of Beach 28 [near Porto Palo].
1600 A/3 - A motor torpedo boat passed close inshore. The enemy vessel was eastbound at high speed.
2046 A/3 - Launched the Folbot. An ASV equipped aircraft patrolling had prevented this from being done earlier.
0135 A/4 - The Folbot returned and HMS Unbending retired to seaward to charge the battery.
0355 A/4 - A diesel engined craft passed eastbound. This may have been a submarine. (11)
4 Mar 1943 During the night of 4/5 March, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) carried out a special operation (reconnaissance of Beach 30 [near Cape San Marco].
During the day HMS Unbending carried out a periscope reconnaissance of Beach 30. On completion of this reconnaissance HMS Unbending bottomed 2 miles to seaward of the beach.
1921 A/4 - Launched the Folbot for a reconnaissance of Beach 30 [near Cape San Marco]. It was manned by Lt. N.W. Cooper, RNVR and Capt. G.W. Burbidge, RCE.

0028 A/5 - Surfaced and started the homing procedure with an infra-red lamp. The weather conditions were deteriorating slowly. Apart from two short dived to avoid detection by ASV equipped aircraft the infra-red lamp was flashing continuously until 0515 A/4.

0430 A/5 - Set course for the alternative rendezvous at 4 knots. The Folbot having failed to return. It had been arranged that if the Folbot failed to return HMS Unbending should proceed to a line 180° from Cape San Marco to proceed up and down between 8 and 12 miles from the coast.
1140 A/5 - In position south of Cape San Marco. Maintaining periscope depth patrol was difficult owing to the weather conditions and this made the possibility of sighting the Folbot poor.
1918 A/5 - Surfaced and continued to patrol the line south of Cape San Marco while flashing the infra-red lamp.
2115 A/5 - Abandoned the search and set course to return to Malta. It is believed the two officers had drowned.
6 Mar 1943 At 1700 A/6, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)

9 Mar 1943 At 1600 A/9, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the area to the south of the Straits of Messina. She carried a Commando of one officer and three other ranks for train wrecking in the Cape Bruzzano tunnel if opportunity offered.

She was routed south of Q.K.B. 6 through position 35°20'N, 15°27'E thence course 010° into the patrol area between latitudes 37°20'N and 38°20'E and west of a line 040° from position 37°20'N, 16°24'E.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (47)

11 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1143 A/11, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); Keep west of 16°00'E during darkness tonight. Friendly MTB's from Malta will be operating to the east of this longtitude. (48)

11 Mar 1943 At 0530 A/11, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0455 A/11 which contained the following orders; Endeavour to be in position 322° - position "SS" - 18 miles by 0800 A/11. [This was a position off the 'toe' of Italy.]
This was however quite impossible as heavy weather had cut down the speed to 7 knots.

At 2100 A/11, HMS Unbending reached the ordered position but aircraft reports by now had indicated that the target, an unescorted tanker, had already passed the position so Lt. Stanley decided to close the coast. (47)
12 Mar 1943 At 0001 A/12, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) established patrol close to the coast between Cape Spartivento and Cape dell'Armi. The swell was still fairly considerable.

At 1200 A/12, the swell started to ease noticeably so course was set for Cape Bruzzano (to the north of Cape Spartivento near position 38°02'N, 16°09'E) with the idea of wrecking a tunnel there, if weather conditions allowed.

At 1915 A/12, on surfacing it was realised that landing would still be difficult so the operation was postponed, and patrol was established in the vicinity of Cape Bruzzano, 5 miles off the coast by night and 2 miles by day. (11)
13 Mar 1943 During the night of 13/14 March 1943, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) landed a raiding party of four men from the Dorsetshire Regiment.

Lt. E.G. Lee, Lance Corporal K. West, Lance Corporal L.R. Hamlin and Private J. O'Brien were to blow up a train in a railway tunnel near Cape Bruzzano, Calabria. The party however failed to return and no explosion was seen on shore. It appears that the four men split, two moved inland and attempted to sabotage the railway line just north of Ferruzzano and the other two went to Brancaleone where they also laid explosives on the railway line. The first pair was captured on 15 March near Fiumarella. The second pair were too late to make their way to the first rendezvous so they hijacked the Italian fishing vessel no.315 from Brancaleone. It had a crew of four and the commandos attempted to meet the submarine at the second rendezvous (090° from Cape Spartivento -3 to 10 miles). This was difficult as the sinking of the Città di Bergamo (see 14 March 1943) had put the Italians on alert. It appears they crossed over to Sicily where they were captured. Meanwhile, the Italians had found one of their Folbots and the explosives on both locations. Those near at Brancaleone were removed but while attempting to deactivate those near Ferrazano, they exploded killing two artificers and destroying the line on a length of four metres but it was quickly repaired.[special thanks to Domenico Clarizia and Francesco Mattesini who helped shed light on this episode].
1500 A/13 to 1800 A/13 - Carried out a careful periscope reconnaissance of the tunnel just inland of Cape Bruzzano which was to be blown up with 60lbs. of TNT, the charge being fired by a train.
It was decided that the Commando's should land just south of the tunnel and overpower the sentry who could be seen there, approaching him openly and stating that the party were Germans.
1810 A/13 - Bottomed 1.5 miles from the shore. Sea conditions were now perfect for Folbot work and the sky was overcast.
1955 A/13 - A destroyer passed to seaward. The enemy vessel was northbound and she was sweeping on Asdic.

2200 A/13 - Surfaced. The moon was giving more light than anticipated, so it was decided to delay the operation for 1.5 hours. As fixing the position was easy, Unbending retired to seaward to charge the battery.
2345 A/13 - Conditions of light were much more suitable now and the sea was nearly flat calm. The two Folbots were launched two miles from the shore.
At 2350 A/13 - The Capt. S.10's signal timed 1048 A/13 was received.

Between 0000 A/14 and 0515 A/14 - HMS Unbending circled four miles from the coast. The Folbots had toches and had been instructed to wave them to seaward intermittently when a mile off shore on the return trip. Nothing was seen of either boat however. Lights were seen flashing on the shore from time to time but no firing was heard and also no explosion was seen or heard.
0515 A/14 - Set course for the alternative day rendezvous on line 090° from Cape Spartivento, 3 to 10 miles from the shore. (11)

13 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1048 A/13, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); Leave patrol as necessary by reverse of outward route to rendezvous with minesweepers at 0830 A/16. HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) is to arrive at same time in accordance with my 1112 A/12. Submarines may be attacked north of 36°00'N. (48)

14 Mar 1943 HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) torpedoed the Italian transport Città di Bergamo (2165 GRT, built 1914) off Cape Spartivento, Italy in position 37°56'N, 16°07'E. She had to be beached but was a constructive total loss, her whole crew was saved.

The convoy attacked was made up of the above-mentioned Città di Bergamo as well as the Italian transports Cosenza (former French Ile Rousse, 1471 GRT, built 1918) and Nino Claudio (2571 GRT, built 1919). They were escorted by the A/S boat VAS 227 and were on passage from Crotone to Messina.
The convoy had sailed from Crotone at 2305 A/13, at 7.5 knots and arrived in Messina at 1800 A/14.
At 1340 A/14, the seaplane 4/189 attacked a submarine and claimed to have sunk it.

At 1800 A/14, the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2201 and UJ 2204 sailed from Messina to hunt the submarine. The Italian auxiliary submarine chaser Quarnaro and VAS 217 and another VAS (so far not identified, perhaps VAS 317 but it is probably a typo) were also sent.

At 2140 A/14, in position 156° - Cape Spartivento - 18 miles, they detected a submarine but at the same time they were attacked by an enemy bomber which dropped two bombs on VAS 217 and machine-gunned it, without causing damage. However, after this attack they had lost contact with the submarine.

1120 A/14 - Sighted a merchant vessel coming down the coast. Started attack. Soon it was seen that a convoy of three merchant vessel came our way. The merchant vessel were of 2000-2500 tons and had an escort of two aircraft and two (or more) motor torpedo boats.

1203 A/14 - In position 37°56'N, 16°07'E fired four torpedoes at two of the merchant vessels that were overlapping. Three torpedo explosions were heard and it was thought that both targets had been hit (which was incorrect).
A counter-attack followed. Ten depth charges were dropped during the next half hour only one at a time. The first two were close.
1255 A/14 - Returned to periscope depth. Only one merchant was in sight hull down to the south-west. Set course for Malta.

1609 A/14 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1208 A/14. As the area was now compromised and the convoy the Capt. S.10 had in mind may well have been the one just attacked, so Lt. Stanley decided to continue proceeding to the southward pending further orders.

2030 A/14 - Received the Capt. S.10's 1604 A/14 and 1912 A/14. The signal timed 1604 A/14 indicated that aircraft would be operating in the vicinity so preparations could made to co-operate with these. No action was however taken on this as it was intended to stay clear of the vicinity of the attack for 24 hours. The second signal gave the position of an airmen in the sea so course was set to search for him. (11)

14 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1208 A/11, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN); Cancel my 1048 A/13. Concentrate your patrol on the toe of Italy until further orders. (49)

15 Mar 1943 At 0100 A/15, in position 37°53'N, 16°34'E, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) picked up Australian flight sergeant Redden, the sole survivor of a Baltimore aircraft shot down by a Me 210.
At 0105 A/15, course was set for Malta as the starboard main motor made it impossible to proceed silently when dived.
At 0300 A/15, passed a signal to the Capt. S.10 reporting the return of HMS Unbending.
At 1400 A/15, the port dynamo, which had been sparking badly, was now found to be in too bad a condition to run. (11)
16 Mar 1943 At 1700 A/16, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)

25 Mar 1943 At 1800 A/25, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet in an area west of 12°00'E and between latitudes 35°45'N and 37°05'N.
En-route to the patrol area, HMS Unbending was to pass between Lampedusa and Linosa.
Lt. Andrew was temporary in command in order to give Lt. Stanley a much needed rest.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (50)

27 Mar 1943 At 1047 A/27, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted the Kuriat lighthouse and set course for a position 10 miles to the north-east of Sousse. The sea state was too rough to patrol at periscope depth.
At 1933 A/27, HMS Unbending surfaced and established patrol 10 miles north-east of Sousse. The town was bombed just after dark. (11)

28 Mar 1943 At 0520 A/28, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) dived and set course 215° to inspect Sousse harbour. Several wrecks were observed inside the harbour. No 'floating' vessel were seen.

At 1937 A/28, HMS Unbending withdrew to a position 10 miles to the north-east of Sousse and patrolled in that position throughout the night. Sousse was bombed at dark and also at dawn the following morning. (11)

29 Mar 1943 At 0750 A/29, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) established patrol 2 miles to the north-east of Sousse harbour. At 0834 A/29, the periscope of HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) was spotted by a shore battery at Sousse and came under fire. The submarine withdrew and set course along the coast to Hergla.
At 1207 A/29, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0954 A/29 was received and course was set to 110° to get south of 35°48'N by dark. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
At 1802 A/29, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1345 A/29 was received. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
At 0055 A/30, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2327 A/29 was received. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]

Presumably these signals contained orders for HMS Unbending in conjunction with an anti-shipping sweep by HMS Paladin (Lt.Cdr. L.St.G. Rich, RN) and RHS Vasilissa Olga (Lt.Cdr. G. Blessas) during the night of 29/30 March. (11)
30 Mar 1943 At 0400 A/30, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) set course 320° for Ras Maamoura and thence to a position north of 36°30'N.
At 1422 A/30, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1148 A/30 was received in which HMS Unbending was ordered to patrol off Sousse. (11)
31 Mar 1943 At 0200 A/31, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) set course 205° for Sousse.
At 1720 A/31, HMS Unbending arrived in a position 3 miles to the north-east of Sousse and commenced to patrol in that area. (11)
2 Apr 1943 At 0511 A/2, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) dived and closed Sousse harbour.

At 0825 A/2, a 2000-tons merchant vessel was observed to be manoeuvring in the harbour. Lt. Andrew had hopes the enemy vessel would come out but she did not do so. The merchant vessel turned round and secured to the northern buoys in the middle of the harbour in such a position that it was impossible to fire a torpedo through the harbour entrance at her.
Perhaps the vessel sighted was the Italian transport Foggia (former French Mont Saint Clair, 1227 GRT, built 1905) which was at Sousse at that time. (11)

2 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 2346 A/2, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) were informed of the following by the Capt. S.10; 1) HMS Abdiel (Capt. D. Orr-Ewing, RN) will be operating along usual submarine route night of 3rd/4th. HMS Unrivalled is to keep 5 miles to northward of route until south of 36°20'N. 2) Waiting destroyers will be operating to the south of Pantellaria, HMS Unbending and HMS Unshaken are to keep west of 12°00'E and south of 36°35'N until daylight/4. 3) Surface forces are aware submarine dispositions and will observe sanctuary rules. (51)

4 Apr 1943 At 1000 A/4, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1321 A/2 which had contained the following orders; Leave patrol passing though position 36°02'N, 12°00'E at 0001 A/5 returning to Malta by reverse of outward route [unknown to us] to arrive at the end of the searched channel at 0830 A/6.

In a signal timed 1004 A/4, the Capt. S.10 sent the folling info to HMS Unbending; My 2346 A/2. Operation will take place tonight. You are to leave patrol as ordered in my 1321 A/2. Surface forces have been informed of your route. (11)

5 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1129 B/5, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN); MTB's will be operating tonight to the west of Malta. HMS Unshaken is to keep south of 36°28'N until 0600 A/6. The MTB's have orders not to attack submarines. (51)
6 Apr 1943 At 1010 A/6, HMS Unbending (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)
12 Apr 1943 At 1600 A/12, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sailed from Malta for Algiers. She was to return to the U.K. for a refit.

She was to take the usual route 'P' but was to keep 5 miles to north and eastward. She was to pass position 'P' at 1600 B/14. [We have been unable to find further details on her initial routing orders after leaving Malta.]

Further routing would be signalled by the Capt. S.8 which he did in a signal timed 1242 A/12 which contained the following orders; Onward routeing through position 38°15'N, 11°18'E at 2100 A/14 and 37°59'N, 04°29'E at 2102 A/17. Thence proceed on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion to make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°57'N, 03°18'E at 0700 A/18.
[No logs are available for this period so no map for the passage back to the U.K. can be displayed.] (52)

13 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 2228 A/13 the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) of the following; 1) Weather permitting two Tribal-class destroyers leaving Bone at 1500 A/14 proceeding north of Galita Islands, thence through position 38°10'N, 10°56'E at 2200 A/14, thence sweeping on line 250° from movement turning back at 2359 A/14 if no target found and returning south of Galita Islands. 2) Two Hunt-class destroyers will carry out a sweep between Galita Islands and 38°10'N until daylight/15. 3) HMS Unison, keep clear to the north-east of these movements. 4) These surface forces are informed on HMS Unbending's movements.
[The two Tribal-class destroyers referred to were HMS Tartar (Cdr. St J.R.J. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN) and HMS Ashanti (Lt.Cdr. J.R. Barnes, RN).]
The two Hunt-class destroyer referred to were HMS Bicester (Lt.Cdr. S.W.F. Bennetts, RN) and HMS Zetland (Lt.Cdr. J.V. Wilkinson, RN).] (53)
14 Apr 1943 At 2315 A/14, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) sighted two friendly destroyers. The submarine changed course to remain out of sight down moon.

The destroyers sighted must have been two out of the following; HMS Pakenham (Cdr. B. Jones, DSC, RN), HMS Paladin (Lt.Cdr. L.St.G. Rich, RN) and HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) which were out on an anti-shipping sweep. (52)
17 Apr 1943 At 1800 A/17, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) established S.S./T. communication with HMS Sahib (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN).
At 2100 A/17, now both on the surface, HMS Unbending sighted HMS Sahib which subsequently reported HMS Unbending as an enemy submarine. (11)
18 Apr 1943 Around 0810 A/18, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) arrived at Algiers from Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Juliet (T/Lt. T. Paxton, RNR) which had joined at 0730 A/18. (11)
19 Apr 1943 Around 1940 A/19, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for Gibraltar.

She was routed as follows; Position 'A' (38°22'N, 02°27'E) at 1130 A/20. Position 'B' (38°22'N, 01°10'E at 0230 A/21. Position 'C' (135° - Cape Palos - 10 miles at 0245 A/22. Position 'D' (180° - Cape de Gaia - 10 miles at 0245 A/23. Position 'E' (180° - Cape Calaburras - 10 miles at 0530 A/24 thence arriving at Gibraltar at 1000 A/24. (11)

21 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1258 B/21, the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar informed HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) of the following; Enemy submarine D/F fixed at 2112 Z/20 and contact attacked by by HMS Cowslip (Lt.Cdr. F. Granger, RNR) which indicated the enemy submarine may be damaged and returning towards HMS Unbending from position 36°37'N, 02°24'W at 2130 B/20.
The enemy submarine fixed by D/F was U-565 which had indeed sent a signal at that time. She was however not attacked and damaged by HMS Cowslip.
[We have been unable to find a report of depth charge attacks by HMS Cowslip.] (54)
24 Apr 1943 At 1030 B/24, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar for Algiers. On arrival she ran over the D/G range. (11)
26 Apr 1943 At 1330 B/26, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for Gosport (Portsmouth).

She was routed as follows; Position 'T' (35°49'N, 06°06'E at 1900 B/26. Position 'U' (35°53'N, 06°15'E at 2000 B/26. Position 'V' (36°37'N, 07°24'E at 0400 B/27. Position 'Y' (36°45'N, 09°20'E at 0230 B/28. Position 'X' (39°08'N, 10°30'E at 1430 B/29. Position 'S' (43°01'N, 10°28'E at 2330 B/1. (55)

28 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 2125 B/28, the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar informed HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) of the following; Enemy submarine sighted by aircraft in position 41°36'N, 09°33'W at 1053 B/28.
[We can not an enemy submarine in this position at this time/date.] (56)

29 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 0931 B/29, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) was informed of the following; U-boat estimated near you today southbound from an aircraft sighting at 0853 Z/28. [We have been unable to find more on this aircraft report and the exact position of this sighting.]

In a signal timed 1523 B/29 the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Unbending; Onward route on route 'Rose' to make rendezvous with the escort in position 'X' (180° - Lizard Head - 12 miles) at 1200 B/7. (56)

7 May 1943 At 1500 A/7, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) made rendezvous with HMS Kingston Topaz (Skr. A. Flett, RNR) which already had HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) in company.
They then set course for Gosport (Portsmouth). (11)
8 May 1943 At 1115 B/8, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) arrived at Gosport (Portsmouth) from Gibraltar. (11)
31 May 1943 At 0935 B/31, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for Plymouth. She was escorted out by HMS Kingston Topaz (Skr. A. Flett, RNR).
HMS Unbending arrived at Plymouth later the same day. (57)
1 Jun 1943 Around 0900 A/1-1000 A/1, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) departed Plymouth for Rothesay. She was escorted by HMS Wanderer (Lt.Cdr. R.F. Whinney, RN). (57)
3 Jun 1943 Around 2359 B/3, HMS Unbending (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) arrived at Rothesay from Plymouth.
As it was not possible for HMS Unbending to start her refit as anticipated she was temporary assigned to training duties. (22)

4 Jun 1943 At 0915 B/4, HMS Unbending (Lt. R.H.H. Brunner, RN) departed Rothesay for Lough Foyle where she was to participate in A/S exercises. She was escorted by HMS Scimitar (Lt.Cdr. C.G. Cuthbertson, DSC, RNR).
They arrived at Lough Foyle later the same day.
Later in June HMS Unbending went to Larne to participate in A/S exercises there. [As no log is available no further details are known.] (58)

30 Jun 1943 HMS Unbending (Lt. R.H.H. Brunner, RN) departed Larne for Plymouth where she finally could commence her refit at the Devonport Dockyard. She was escorted by HMS Sardonyx (Lt.Cdr. A.F.C. Gray, RNR). (59)
1 Jul 1943 HMS Unbending (Lt. R.H.H. Brunner, RN) arrived at Plymouth from Larne. Refit was commenced on 6 July. (22)
9 Nov 1943 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted a basis dive at the Devonport Dockyard. (60)
13 Nov 1943 With her refit completed, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted D/G and compass adjustment trials off Plymouth. (60)
14 Nov 1943 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted post refit diving and gunnery trials off Plymouth. (60)
15 Nov 1943 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) proceeded from Plymouth to Dartmouth. Also log trials were carried out. She was escorted by HMS Fir (T/Skr. W.H. Buley, RNR). (60)
17 Nov 1943 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) proceeded from Dartmouth to Plymouth. She returned to the Devonport Dockyard for some repairs and to have a new forward periscope fitted. (60)
5 Dec 1943 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted trials exercises off Plymouth. She was escorted by HMS Mazurka (A/Skr.Lt. J. Baxter, RNR). (61)
8 Dec 1943 Around 1440 A/8, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) departed Plymouth for Fishguard. She was escorted by HMS Mazurka (A/Skr.Lt. J. Baxter, RNR). (61)
9 Dec 1943 Around 2045 A/9, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) arrived at Fishguard. She had parted company with her escort, HMS Mazurka (A/Skr.Lt. J. Baxter, RNR), around 1815 A/9. (61)
13 Dec 1943 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Fishguard with aircraft. (61)

3 Jan 1944 Around 1800 A/3, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) departed Fishguard for Rothesay. During the passage north through the Irish Sea she was escorted by HMS Kihna (A/Cdr.(Retd.) A.R.W. Sayle, RD, RNR). (62)
5 Jan 1944 Around 0220 A/5, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) arrived at Rothesay. (62)
10 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted D/F calibration trials off Rothesay. (62)
11 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S and attack exercises in the Clyde area with ORP Dragon (Cdr. S.T. Dzienisiewicz). (62)
12 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (62)
13 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) proceeded from Loch Goil to Rothesay. (62)

14 Jan 1944 At 2100 A/14, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) departed Rothesay for Londonderry / Lough Foyle. During the passage she was escorted by HMS Kihna (A/Cdr.(Retd.) A.R.W. Sayle, RD, RNR). (62)
15 Jan 1944 Around 1200 A/15, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) arrived at Lough Foyle where she was to be employed as A/S target for escort ships.
She had parted company with her escort, HMS Kihna (A/Cdr.(Retd.) A.R.W. Sayle, RD, RNR) around 0840 A/15. (62)
17 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Lough Foyle with (at least) HMS Scarborough (Lt.Cdr. E.B. Carnduff, RN). (62)

19 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) proceeded from Moville to Londonderry. (62)
21 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Lough Foyle with (at least) HMS Lossie (Lt.Cdr. A.F. MacFie, OBE, RNR). (62)
22 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Lough Foyle with (at least) HMS Erne (Lt.Cdr. W.R. Hickey, RNR) and HMS Churchill (Lt. A.C.D. Leach, RN). (62)

23 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Lough Foyle with (at least) HMS Erne (Lt.Cdr. W.R. Hickey, RNR), HMCS Fennel (A/Lt.Cdr. W.P. Moffat, RCNVR) and HMS Vanoc (Lt.Cdr. P.R. Ward, RN). (62)

24 Jan 1944 HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Lough Foyle with (at least) HMS Mourne (Lt.Cdr. R.S. Holland, RD, RNR) and HMS Clematis (T/Lt. R.H. Farrands, DSC, RNR). (62)
26 Jan 1944 At 1540 A/26, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) departed Lough Foyle for Blyth. She was escorted by HMS Kihna (A/Cdr.(Retd.) A.R.W. Sayle, RD, RNR). (62)
29 Jan 1944 Around 1645 A/29, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) arrived at Blyth from Lough Foyle. (62)

16 Mar 1944 Around 0630 A/16, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) and HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) departed Blyth for Lerwick.

The Admiralty was under the impression that the damaged German battleship Tirpitz might be leaving Norway for Germany to effect repairs. So a whole lot of submarines were sent to patrol off Norway including several submarines from training flotillas. (11)
17 Mar 1944 At 1650 A/17, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) arrived at Lerwick from Blyth. (11)
18 Mar 1944 Around 1515 A/18, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) departed Lerwick for her 18th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off Utvaer, Norway (area 'T18A').
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unbending during this patrol see the map below.
21 Mar 1944 At 0100 A/21, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 2308 A/20 was recalled from patrol and was to return to Lerwick.
At 1810 A/21, HMS Unbending ended her 18th war patrol at Lerwick. (11)

24 Mar 1944 Around 0030 A/24, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN), HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) and HMS Trusty (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Lerwick for Blyth or Dundee (HMS Trusty). They were escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (11)
